title: Beyond Outliers: A Study of Optimizers Under Quantization
author: Georgios Vlassis et al
description: This paper is the first large-scale study of how optimizer choice affects both post-training quantization (PTQ) and quantization-aware training (QAT), training language models from 50M to 1.5B parameters with six optimizers and comparing their behavior before and after 4-bit quantization. To make the comparison fair, the authors first perform extensive hyperparameter sweeps for each optimizer, train all models to a common validation loss using Chinchilla-optimal compute, then apply uniform W4A4 PTQ or retrain with a state-of-the-art 4-bit QAT method (QuEST), allowing them to isolate the effect of the optimizer itself on quantization robustness. For PTQ, they discover that commonly used outlier metrics such as Max-to-Median Ratio (MMR) and kurtosis do not reliably predict quantized model quality across optimizers, even though these metrics are often assumed to explain quantization failure. To explain this, they develop a theoretical framework that tracks quantization error propagation through the network by decomposing activation distortion into three components: accumulated error from previous layers (A), newly introduced layer-wise quantization error (B), and their interaction (C), producing a relative-error metric R that measures how far the quantized network deviates from the original model at every layer. Their analysis shows that overall quantization performance is dominated not by local layer outliers but by how errors are amplified and propagated through successive layers, and they further decompose this amplification into spectral and alignment factors to identify why some optimizers are more robust than others. Empirically, they find that Muon is typically strongest in full-precision training, whereas Shampoo consistently exhibits the smallest degradation under both PTQ and QAT, and scaling-law analysis reveals that Shampoo achieves the highest parameter efficiency under quantization, suggesting its advantages persist as model size increases.

title: CDLM: Consistency Diffusion Language Models for Faster Sampling
author: Minseo Kim et al
description: CDLM introduces a post-training recipe for diffusion language models that attacks both major inference bottlenecks simultaneously: the large number of denoising iterations and the inability to use standard KV caching under bidirectional attention. The method first generates offline decoding trajectories from a pretrained bidirectional diffusion language model teacher, recording not only intermediate partially-masked sequences but also the hidden states associated with finalized tokens, then initializes a student model with the teacher’s weights and replaces full bidirectional attention with a block-wise causal mask so completed blocks can be cached during inference. Training combines three objectives: a distillation loss that teaches the student to predict multiple future token finalizations using teacher logits reconstructed from stored hidden states, a consistency loss that forces predictions from an earlier partially-refined state to match predictions from a later block-completed state, and a standard masked-denoising objective that preserves the model’s original diffusion capabilities. The consistency component effectively adapts the idea of consistency models from continuous diffusion systems to discrete token generation by encouraging the student to make larger jumps along the decoding trajectory rather than refining one token at a time. At inference, the student decodes block-by-block using exact KV caching, while confidence-thresholded parallel finalization allows multiple masked tokens inside a block to be revealed in a single iteration, substantially reducing the number of refinement steps required. The main contribution is showing that consistency-guided distillation and block-causal training can be combined into a single framework that preserves much of the generation quality of diffusion language models while achieving significantly faster sampling and better hardware utilization.

title: Residual Context Diffusion Language Models
author: Yuezhoe Hu et al
description: This paper introduces Residual Context Diffusion (RCD), a modification to diffusion language models that reuses information from low-confidence tokens that are normally discarded during remasking, arguing that these tokens already contain useful semantic and contextual signals accumulated during previous denoising steps. Instead of resetting unresolved positions back to a fixed mask embedding, RCD converts each token’s predicted probability distribution into a soft embedding by taking the weighted average of the model’s vocabulary embeddings, producing a residual context vector that summarizes the model’s uncertainty and partial knowledge at that position. To avoid overwhelming the original input representation, the residual vector is injected through a highway-style interpolation mechanism, where the mixing coefficient is determined by the token’s normalized entropy, giving greater influence to uncertain predictions that potentially contain richer information for future refinement. Training such a recurrent residual-feedback loop directly would require expensive backpropagation through multiple denoising iterations, so the authors introduce a two-stage pipeline: first training a frozen reference diffusion model to generate stable probability distributions, then training a target model to consume residual contexts derived from those distributions as supervised hints. During inference, the target model recursively generates its own residuals, applies temperature-scaled entropy calibration to align residual statistics with those seen during training, and feeds the resulting contextual embeddings into subsequent denoising iterations, effectively recycling computation that standard diffusion decoding throws away. The main contribution is showing that information lost through remasking is a significant bottleneck in diffusion language models, and that preserving and propagating this discarded probabilistic context yields substantially better reasoning quality and accuracy–latency tradeoffs with only minimal additional computational overhead.

title: The Synergy of Speculative Decoding and Batching in Serving Large Language Models
author: Qidong Sun et al
description: This paper studies how batching and speculative decoding interact during LLM serving, showing that these two acceleration techniques are not independent and that the optimal speculative decoding configuration changes as batch size changes. The authors implement a batched speculative decoding system for HuggingFace models, where a small speculative model (SSM) autoregressively proposes multiple future tokens, while the large model (LLM) verifies all proposed tokens in parallel using attention masking and discards incorrect continuations, allowing multiple tokens to be accepted in a single LLM pass. Through extensive profiling, they discover that larger batch sizes require shorter speculation lengths because batching already improves GPU utilization, making the additional parallelism from long speculative sequences less beneficial and sometimes harmful due to verification overhead. To explain this behavior, they build an analytical runtime model that combines LLM verification cost, SSM generation cost, and the expected number of accepted speculative tokens, showing mathematically that the speculation length that minimizes latency decreases as batch size increases. Based on this insight, they propose adaptive speculative decoding, which performs an offline profiling phase to build a lookup table mapping each batch size to its optimal speculation length, and then dynamically selects that length at runtime for each incoming batch. Their main contribution is demonstrating that speculation length should be treated as a workload-dependent serving parameter rather than a fixed hyperparameter, and that adapting it to current batching conditions consistently yields better latency and throughput trade-offs than static speculative decoding strategies.

title: Enhancing Reasoning for Diffusion LLMs via Distribution Matching Policy Optimization
author: Yuchen Zhu et al
description: This paper introduces Distribution Matching Policy Optimization (DMPO), a reinforcement learning framework specifically designed for diffusion LLMs that replaces the conventional objective of maximizing expected reward with matching the model’s output distribution to an optimal reward-weighted target distribution. Instead of pushing the model toward only the highest-reward responses as PPO-, GRPO-, and diffu-GRPO-style methods do, DMPO constructs a target distribution proportional to the pretrained model probability multiplied by an exponential reward term, allowing the model to learn a diverse set of high-quality reasoning trajectories rather than collapsing onto a single reward mode. To implement this, the authors derive a Weighted Denoising Cross-Entropy (WDCE) loss: responses are sampled from a reference policy, assigned importance weights computed from reward and likelihood ratios, and then trained using the standard diffusion denoising objective, effectively turning RL into weighted supervised denoising. A key advantage is that WDCE is both off-policy and forward-only: it does not require expensive trajectory likelihood estimation or on-policy rollouts during optimization, enabling replay-buffer reuse, greater sample efficiency, and compatibility with accelerated diffusion inference methods. The authors identify that small rollout batches can cause all samples to receive positive training weights, which may incorrectly reinforce poor responses, and address this through a novel weight-baseline subtraction mechanism that introduces negative gradients by comparing each sample against group-, individual-, or model-based baselines. They further extend the framework with Weighted Direct Discriminative Optimization (WDDO), a GAN-inspired objective that naturally incorporates positive and negative preference signals, and show that these distribution-matching techniques substantially improve reasoning performance while remaining computationally efficient and stable under highly stale off-policy data.

title: Continuous Diffusion Scales Competitively with Discrete Diffusion for Language
author: Zhihan Yang et al
description: This paper introduces RePlaid, a redesigned version of the continuous diffusion language model Plaid, and shows that continuous diffusion can scale much more competitively than previously believed when evaluated under the same architecture, optimization settings, and scaling-law protocol as modern discrete diffusion language models. The core method represents text tokens as low-dimensional learnable embeddings, applies Gaussian diffusion directly in this continuous embedding space, and trains a transformer denoiser using a likelihood-based variational objective (ELBO), where the model predicts clean token embeddings from noisy latent representations while simultaneously learning both the embedding geometry and the diffusion noise schedule. To create RePlaid, the authors preserved Plaid’s probabilistic diffusion framework but modernized the architecture to match contemporary diffusion transformers, incorporating features such as bidirectional attention, adaptive normalization, self-conditioning, and improved numerical implementations, enabling a fair comparison with recent discrete diffusion models. A key contribution is demonstrating that optimizing the ELBO naturally learns a noise schedule that distributes denoising difficulty nearly uniformly across time, producing an approximately linear information-loss trajectory without requiring the handcrafted time reparameterizations used in many previous continuous diffusion methods. The authors further show that likelihood-based training shapes the token embedding space into a structured, low-rank geometry that improves modeling efficiency, whereas adding discriminative cross-entropy objectives tends to disperse embeddings and degrade likelihood performance. Overall, the paper argues that the combination of learnable embeddings, variance-optimized noise schedules, self-conditioning, and principled likelihood training allows continuous diffusion models to achieve scaling behavior, generation quality, and parameter efficiency comparable to strong discrete diffusion approaches while retaining the advantages of continuous latent spaces.

title: Planned Diffusion
author: Daniel Israel et al
description: Planned Diffusion introduces a hybrid language model that combines autoregressive planning with discrete diffusion generation, allowing the model to learn its own denoising order instead of relying on hand-designed heuristics. The core idea is that the model first generates a sequence of control tags describing semantically independent chunks of the future response, including a short topic description and an estimated length for each chunk, effectively producing a plan that partitions the output into parallelizable regions. At inference time, the runtime converts this plan into masked spans, and the diffusion model fills all chunks simultaneously, while performing an independent denoising schedule within each chunk; this means latency depends primarily on the longest chunk rather than the total output length. To support this behavior, the authors train a single transformer with a mixed objective: planning tokens are optimized autoregressively with causal attention, while content tokens are optimized using a diffusion denoising loss with bidirectional attention, enabling both modes to coexist within one model. The training data is automatically annotated with special planning and chunking tags, and the model learns not only to generate text but also to decide how that text should be decomposed into semantically independent units before generation begins. The main contribution is showing that denoising order can be reframed as a learned planning problem, producing a simple single-model system that exploits semantic parallelism during generation without requiring a separate draft model, verifier model, or manually designed decoding schedule.

title: Large Language Models Can Self-Improve
author: Jiaxin Huang et al
description: The paper proposes that large language models can improve their own reasoning ability without labeled data by generating and training on their own solutions. The method uses Chain-of-Thought prompting to sample multiple reasoning paths per question, then applies self-consistency via majority voting to identify high-confidence answers. Only the reasoning paths that lead to the majority answer are kept, forming pseudo-labeled training data consisting of both intermediate reasoning and final answers. The model is then fine-tuned on this self-generated dataset using a mixture of prompt and output formats to avoid overfitting to a single style and to encourage both step-by-step and direct-answer reasoning. A key insight is that confidence from agreement across sampled reasoning paths serves as an effective proxy for correctness, allowing noisy self-labels to still improve performance. Overall, the technique reframes self-training for LLMs by leveraging internal reasoning diversity and consistency as a signal for learning, rather than relying on external supervision.

title: Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence
author: Kimi Team
description: Kimi K2 is a Mixture-of-Experts large language model with 1.Kimi K2 is a Mixture-of-Experts large language model with 1.04 trillion total parameters and 32 billion activated parameters, designed specifically to advance agentic intelligence — the ability of models to autonomously plan, reason, and act across complex environments. The central training innovation is MuonClip, a new optimizer that combines the token-efficient Muon algorithm with a mechanism called QK-Clip, which prevents attention logit explosion during large-scale training by rescaling query and key projection weights on a per-head basis whenever logits exceed a threshold, enabling stable training across 15.5 trillion tokens without a single loss spike. To improve how much learning signal each token contributes, the team developed domain-specialized data rephrasing pipelines that rewrite high-quality knowledge and mathematics documents in varied styles and formats, demonstrating that diverse rephrasings outperform simply repeating the same data across multiple epochs. For post-training, a large-scale agentic data synthesis pipeline was built to generate tens of thousands of tool-use training trajectories by constructing a repository of over 3,000 real MCP tools and 20,000 synthetic tools, pairing them with generated agents and tasks, simulating multi-turn interactions through a stateful tool execution environment, and filtering trajectories with an LLM judge. Reinforcement learning is extended beyond verifiable tasks through a self-critique rubric reward mechanism, where the model evaluates its own outputs via pairwise comparisons against a combination of core, prescriptive, and human-annotated rubrics, with the critic continuously refined using signals from verifiable tasks to ground its subjective judgments. Additional RL training improvements include a per-sample token budget with truncation penalties to promote concise outputs, a PTX auxiliary loss over curated high-quality data to prevent forgetting, and a temperature decay schedule that transitions from broad exploration early in training to stable exploitation later.

title: Efficient Expert Pruning for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Language Models: Enhancing Performance and Reducing Inference Costs
author: Enshu Liu et al
description: The paper proposes Efficient Expert Pruning (EEP), a gradient-free evolutionary method for compressing sparse Mixture-of-Experts language models by jointly optimizing which experts to keep and how to combine them. The core technique introduces two learnable matrices, a router mapping matrix and an expert merging matrix, which define a continuous search space that can represent both discrete pruning (via one-hot selection) and soft merging of experts. EEP operates in two phases: first, it performs structured expert pruning by searching for an optimal subset of experts without modifying model weights, and second, it merges pruned knowledge back into remaining experts by linearly combining their parameters. The optimization is performed using an evolutionary strategy that iteratively selects, mutates, and recombines candidate pruning/merging configurations based solely on inference performance, avoiding any gradient computation or backpropagation. This design allows the method to run under inference-only constraints while still effectively “fine-tuning” the model through weight-space transformations rather than parameter updates. A key insight is that redundancy across experts enables aggressive pruning, and that merging acts as a form of knowledge consolidation analogous to ensembling in weight space. Overall, the novelty lies in framing expert pruning and adaptation as a unified, gradient-free search over structured transformations that simultaneously reduce model size, adjust routing behavior, and preserve or even improve task performance.

title: Which Heads Matter for Reasoning? RL-Guided KV Cache Compression
author: Wenjie Du et al
description: The paper identifies a core failure of existing KV cache compression methods in reasoning LLMs: they treat all attention heads uniformly or target retrieval heads, which breaks chain-of-thought consistency because the KV cache in reasoning models encodes intermediate reasoning states rather than just retrieval context. Its main novelty is RLKV, which uses reinforcement learning as a probing mechanism to directly measure how compressing each attention head affects end-to-end reasoning quality, rather than relying on proxy metrics like attention scores or next-token loss. Concretely, the method inserts per-head gating parameters that interpolate between full KV attention and compressed local attention, and optimizes these gates using RL with reward signals derived from actual generation correctness, combined with an L1 sparsity penalty to force most heads toward compression. This produces a sparse solution in which a small subset of “reasoning-critical heads” retain full KV cache while the rest use a constant-size cache (sink tokens plus recent window), yielding a structured head-level compression policy. A key technical challenge is instability from sparse rewards versus dense regularization, which the paper addresses with self-distillation sampling to stabilize rewards and adaptive penalty weighting that modulates the L1 term based on current performance. At inference, the learned continuous gates are discretized into a top-k selection of heads that keep full KV cache, resulting in a hybrid attention system that preserves reasoning dynamics while significantly reducing memory and computation. Overall, the technique reframes KV cache compression as a policy learning problem over heads, revealing that reasoning behavior is localized to specific heads and can be preserved by selectively maintaining their full attention context.

title: dMoE: dLLMs with Learnable Block Experts
author: Sicheng Feng et al
description: The paper identifies a fundamental inefficiency in combining diffusion language models (dLLMs) with mixture-of-experts (MoE): while dLLMs process multiple tokens jointly in a block, standard MoE performs token-level routing, leading to a large number of uniquely activated experts and a memory-bound inference bottleneck. To address this mismatch, the authors propose dMoE, which replaces token-level expert selection with a block-level routing strategy by aggregating token-wise router scores into a single block-level expert distribution. This aggregated distribution is then used to select a small “coreset” of experts via a top-p criterion, after which standard token-level routing is constrained to only this subset, preserving per-token computation while reducing global expert diversity. A key technical insight is that token-level router scores correlate with expert importance, making simple aggregation effective, and that expert concentration varies across blocks and denoising steps, motivating adaptive (rather than fixed-size) expert selection. The method is trained using a self-distillation framework that applies the same constrained routing during forward passes, ensuring consistency between training and inference without modifying the underlying model architecture. Overall, the novelty lies in reframing MoE routing from a token-centric to a block-centric perspective tailored to diffusion decoding, enabling substantial reduction in activated experts and memory overhead while maintaining the original model’s behavior.

title: Self Forcing: Bridging the Train-Test Gap in Autoregressive Video Diffusion
author: Xun Huang et al
description: Self Forcing introduces a training paradigm for autoregressive video diffusion models that explicitly addresses exposure bias arising from the mismatch between training on ground-truth context and inference on self-generated context. Instead of conditioning on real or noise-corrupted frames as in Teacher Forcing or Diffusion Forcing, it performs full autoregressive rollout during training, so each frame is generated from the model’s own previous outputs, aligning the training distribution with the inference distribution. This enables the use of holistic, video-level distribution matching objectives that optimize the joint sequence distribution rather than independent per-frame denoising losses. To make this computationally feasible, the method relies on few-step diffusion along with stochastic gradient truncation, so gradients are only propagated through a limited portion of the denoising and temporal chain. The framework also incorporates KV caching during training, mirroring inference-time computation and allowing efficient sequential generation without specialized masking. Finally, a rolling KV cache mechanism is introduced to support long-horizon generation with constant memory and without recomputation, while additional training constraints ensure stability under this shifting context.

title: Autoregressive Image Generation without Vector Quantization
author: Tianhong Li et al
description: The paper challenges the assumption that autoregressive image generation requires discrete tokenization, arguing that the essential requirement is modeling a conditional distribution over tokens rather than enforcing categorical structure. It proposes replacing categorical cross-entropy with a diffusion-based loss that models the per-token conditional distribution in continuous space, using a denoising process conditioned on autoregressive features. In this framework, the autoregressive model predicts a latent conditioning vector, and a small diffusion model defines a flexible distribution over the next continuous token, enabling sampling via reverse diffusion. This decouples sequence modeling from the choice of token representation and removes the need for vector quantization, which is known to introduce reconstruction error and training instability. The method also unifies standard autoregressive and masked generative models under a generalized autoregressive formulation, where prediction order can be arbitrary and multiple tokens can be generated simultaneously. Conceptually, the approach separates modeling of inter-token dependencies (handled by autoregression) from modeling of token-level uncertainty (handled by diffusion), providing a more expressive and modular probabilistic framework.

title: Mean Flows for One-Step Generative Modeling
author: Zhengyang Geng et al
description: The paper addresses the difficulty of one-step generative modeling, where existing diffusion and flow-based approaches rely on modeling instantaneous velocity fields and require integrating them over time, which is inherently multi-step and leads to inaccuracies when approximated coarsely. It introduces the concept of average velocity as a fundamental object, defined as the time-averaged integral of the instantaneous velocity, and treats this as a new ground-truth field for learning. The key theoretical contribution is the MeanFlow identity, which establishes an exact relationship between average velocity, instantaneous velocity, and the time derivative of the average velocity, enabling training without explicitly computing integrals. Using this identity, the method constructs a self-consistent training objective where a neural network learns the average velocity field by matching a target derived from instantaneous velocity and a Jacobian-vector product term. This formulation avoids heuristic consistency constraints used in prior work and instead derives consistency properties directly from the underlying definition of average velocity, making the framework principled and model-independent. As a result, the approach enables direct approximation of the full transport in a single evaluation, since the average velocity inherently encodes the integrated effect of the flow over time.

title: Accelerated Sampling from Masked Diffusion Models via Entropy Bounded Unmasking
author: Heli Ben-Hamu et al
description: The paper addresses the inefficiency of sampling in masked diffusion models, where standard methods either unmask one token at a time or unmask multiple tokens independently at the cost of significant error. It observes that model predictions often simultaneously exhibit low entropy and low local error for multiple tokens, implying that some tokens can be safely unmasked in parallel without introducing substantial dependence errors. Based on this, the authors propose EB-Sampler, an adaptive sampling method that dynamically selects both the order and number of tokens to unmask using an entropy-based criterion that bounds joint dependence. The method is grounded in a theoretical decomposition of sampling error into two components: model error from imperfect conditional predictions and joint dependence error from incorrectly assuming independence between tokens. By using entropy as a proxy for dependence, EB-Sampler controls the second error term while leveraging existing heuristics to minimize the first, yielding a principled rule for multi-token unmasking. The approach is further formalized as part of a broader class of adaptive samplers, showing that efficient and correct sampling can be achieved without retraining the underlying model by appropriately designing the unmasking policy.

title: DPad: Efficient Diffusion Language Models with Suffix Dropout
author: Xinhua Chen et al
description: Diffusion language models achieve parallel generation by predicting all suffix tokens at each denoising step, but this introduces a fundamental inefficiency because most suffix computations are discarded and contribute little to the final output. The paper identifies that suffix tokens primarily act as a scratchpad: an information reservoir that aggregates signals from prefix and current tokens across layers and feeds them back in a write–store–read cycle, rather than carrying meaningful standalone semantics. From this perspective, the authors argue that suffix attention is inherently sparse and dominated by nearby tokens, with distant tokens being redundant and largely position-insensitive. Based on this, they propose DPad, a training-free method that enforces structured sparsity via a fixed-length sliding window and a distance-decay dropout that removes far suffix tokens before attention is computed, thereby reducing complexity without altering model parameters. The method is theoretically motivated by the observation that the scratchpad behaves like a low-rank residual pathway, implying that only a small subset of suffix tokens is sufficient to preserve the necessary contextual information for denoising. Finally, they formalize this intuition through the Diffusion Lottery Tickets hypothesis, which posits that a sparse, dynamically selected subset of suffix tokens can act as “winning tickets” that maintain generation quality, making aggressive pruning both feasible and principled.

title: Group Sequence Policy Optimization
author: Chujie Zheng et al
description: GRPO's instability — especially severe in long-response and MoE training — stems from a fundamental misapplication of importance sampling: applying per-token importance ratios to single samples from each next-token distribution doesn't perform valid distribution correction, it just introduces high-variance noise that accumulates over sequence length and compounds through the clipping mechanism. GSPO fixes this by defining the importance ratio at the sequence level as the geometric mean of per-token likelihood ratios, which is theoretically grounded, length-normalized, and naturally aligned with the fact that rewards are granted to whole sequences rather than individual tokens. A key insight from the gradient analysis is that GRPO's instability comes from unequal per-token weighting that can vary wildly, whereas GSPO weights all tokens in a response equally, eliminating this source of noise. A particularly valuable downstream benefit is that sequence-level importance ratios are insensitive to MoE expert-routing volatility between gradient updates, eliminating the need for Routing Replay workarounds and enabling the full model capacity to be utilized — which directly contributed to the Qwen3 model improvements.

title: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
author: John Schulman et al
description: Standard policy gradient methods can only safely take one gradient step per data sample, since larger updates risk destructively large policy changes, while the principled fix (TRPO) requires expensive second-order optimization and is incompatible with parameter sharing or dropout. PPO's key insight is that clipping the probability ratio r(θ) = π_θ/π_θ_old into [1-ε, 1+ε] and taking the minimum of the clipped and unclipped objectives creates a simple, first-order pessimistic lower bound on policy performance — the clip removes the incentive to move too far from the old policy, and the min ensures the bound is never optimistic. This allows multiple epochs of minibatch SGD on the same rollout data without catastrophic policy degradation, achieving TRPO-like stability with only a few lines of code change over vanilla policy gradient. Empirically PPO outperforms TRPO, A2C, and other baselines on continuous control benchmarks while being far simpler to implement, making it the practical default that later work like GRPO directly builds upon.

title: LoRA Without Regret
author: John Schulman et al
description: This paper from Thinking Machines finds that LoRA can match full fine-tuning (FullFT) performance when applied to all network layers — especially MLP/MoE layers — rather than just attention layers, which consistently underperform even when given equivalent parameter counts. LoRA's effectiveness holds as long as its trainable parameter count exceeds the information content of the training data, a condition easily met for most post-training scenarios. For reinforcement learning specifically, even rank-1 LoRA fully matches FullFT because policy gradient methods absorb only ~1 bit of information per episode, making LoRA's capacity more than sufficient. Practically, the optimal learning rate for LoRA is consistently ~10x higher than for FullFT, and LoRA also uses only ~⅔ the compute FLOPs per pass, giving it a meaningful efficiency advantage.

title: Defeating Nondeterminisim in LLM Inference
author: Horace He et al
description: LLM inference nondeterminism is widely attributed to concurrent GPU threads and floating-point arithmetic, but the real culprit is actually batch non-invariance — the mathematical result for a single user's request silently changes depending on how many other users are being served simultaneously. Since floating-point addition is non-associative, changing the batch size changes the reduction order, which changes the numerical output — and because server load is unpredictable, results become nondeterministic from the user's perspective even though the server itself is technically deterministic given fixed inputs. The authors resolve this by engineering batch-invariant kernels for the three key operations (RMSNorm, matrix multiplication, and attention), ensuring each element's reduction order is identical regardless of batch size, at a ~60% latency cost that can be partially recovered with kernel optimization. The most striking practical implication is for reinforcement learning: because training and inference normally produce different logprobs for the same sequence, on-policy RL is silently off-policy — but with bitwise-identical deterministic inference, true on-policy RL becomes achievable, eliminating the need for importance weighting corrections entirely.

title: Modular Manifolds
author: Jeremy Bernstein et al
description: Neural network training benefits from keeping tensors "healthy" in size, and while normalization is standard for activations and gradients, constraining weight matrices to manifolds — curved surfaces defined by geometric properties — is a promising but underexplored approach. The article derives a "Manifold Muon" optimizer that keeps weight matrices on the Stiefel manifold (matrices whose singular values are all exactly 1), meaning the weights never stretch or shrink input vectors unevenly, which is achieved by framing the optimization step as a convex dual problem solved via gradient ascent. The deeper insight is that geometry shapes the optimal update direction: by choosing a non-Euclidean distance measure (like the spectral norm), the best gradient step is no longer simply "follow the gradient" — it's a spectrally normalized direction that maximally reduces the loss within a constrained step size. The post concludes with the concept of modular manifolds, a composable framework where individual layers each carry their own manifold constraint and norm, and combining layers automatically propagates Lipschitz sensitivity bounds that intelligently budget learning rates across the whole network — making optimizer design as principled and automatic as the forward pass itself.

title: Soft-Masked Diffusion Language Models
author: Michael Hersche et al
description: The paper introduces soft-masking (SM), a mechanism that replaces the binary mask-or-unmask decision in masked diffusion language models with a confidence-weighted blend of the [MASK] token and the top-k predicted tokens, allowing partial information to propagate across decoding steps rather than being discarded. Implementation is lightweight, requiring only three additional trainable parameters and a two-pass training procedure — a gradient-free first pass to generate soft-masked representations, followed by a second pass that updates both the backbone and SM parameters — preserving full parallelism across sequence length. A key conceptual insight is that SM can be understood as a smooth interpolation between standard masked diffusion and uniform diffusion in embedding space, and that retaining a portion of the mask token is itself useful since many models are pretrained to recognize it as a structural signal. Crucially, SM is modular and composable — it integrates cleanly with existing efficiency techniques like advanced unmasking schedulers and blockwise caching, and can be grafted onto already-pretrained models via short continued training rather than requiring a full retraining run.

title: Coevolutionary Continuous Discrete Diffusion: Make Your Diffusion Language Model a Latent Reasoner
author: Cai Zhou et al
description: CCDD is motivated by a theoretical proof that continuous diffusion is strictly more expressive than discrete diffusion (and can even simulate looped transformers), yet historically underperforms in practice — a contradiction the paper attributes to three trainability failures: a large and poorly structured decision space, atomic non-smooth generation targets, and combinatorial decoding ambiguity when mapping continuous representations back to tokens. The core insight resolving this tension is that discrete and continuous modalities are complementary: the discrete masked diffusion path anchors decoding and reduces input uncertainty, while the continuous path (operating over contextualized embeddings from a pretrained encoder like Qwen3-Embedding) preserves rich semantic history across denoising steps that would otherwise be discarded. Implementation runs a factored joint forward process — independent Gaussian noise on the continuous side and CTMC masking on the discrete side — with a single shared transformer receiving both modalities as input and optimizing a weighted sum of MSE and cross-entropy losses, with extras like asynchronous noise schedules (continuous decays slower, acting as implicit planning guidance for discrete decoding) and classifier-free guidance treating the continuous stream as self-generated conditioning. The result is a model that generalizes CDMs, DDMs, and looped transformers under one framework, achieving over 25% perplexity reduction versus a same-scale MDLM baseline on LM1B — suggesting that grounding continuous latent reasoning in parallel discrete tokens is a principled path to closing the gap between theoretical expressivity and practical performance in diffusion language models.

title: Soft Thinking: Unlocking the Reasoning Potential of LLMs in Continuous Concept Space
author: Zhen Zhang et al
description: Soft Thinking challenges the assumption that LLMs must reason through discrete language tokens, instead proposing that each reasoning step produce a "concept token" — a probability-weighted mixture of all token embeddings — allowing the model to inhabit a continuous concept space rather than committing to a single word at each step. This preserves the full probability distribution across the vocabulary at every intermediate step, which theoretically approximates marginalizing over all possible reasoning paths simultaneously, rather than greedily sampling one trajectory. To prevent the model from drifting into out-of-distribution collapse (since it was never trained on these continuous inputs), the authors introduce a "Cold Stop" mechanism that monitors output entropy and terminates reasoning early when the model becomes consistently confident. The result is a training-free method that plugs into any existing CoT pipeline, improves reasoning by enabling implicit parallel path exploration, and produces more concise outputs because richer per-token semantics reduce the number of steps needed to reach a conclusion.

title: Latent-Augmented Discrete Diffusion Models
author: Dario Shariatian et al
description: The core insight is that masked discrete diffusion models are fundamentally bottlenecked by their factorized reverse transitions, which treat each token independently — a tolerable assumption when denoising one token at a time, but increasingly damaging when many tokens must be unmasked simultaneously, since independent commitments can produce globally incoherent sequences. LADD addresses this by introducing an auxiliary latent channel that runs alongside the token channel, carrying cross-token structural information and providing a secondary conditioning signal that makes the per-token factorization a better approximation of the true joint reverse distribution. The framework is instantiated in two flavors — continuous latents (Co-LADD) and discrete latents (Di-LADD) — each combinable with either a joint denoising strategy (tokens and latents co-evolve together) or a sequential strategy (latents are resolved first, then condition the entire token unmasking pass), with principled ELBO objectives derived for all combinations. The key practical finding is that discrete latents with joint co-evolution tend to perform best at moderate-to-large sampling budgets, while sequential inference offers the most benefit at very small step counts by eliminating early-stage ambiguity before aggressive multi-token unmasking begins.

title: A Theory on Adam Instability in Large-Scale Machine Learning
author: Igor Molybog et al
description: Authors see that Adam sometimes suffers from a large norm in the parameter update vector, becoming uncorrelated with the direction of descent, and note that this is more likely to be seen when training deep models with large batch sizes. They argue that teh instabilities are associated with time-domain correlation between grad estimates of earlier layers.

title: Adam-Mini: Use Fewer Learning Rates to Gain More
author: Yushun Zhang et al
description: The authors propose an optimizer (AdamMini) that cutes down the learning rate resources used in memory, by partitioning the parameters of v into blocks based on the Hessian. They then assign a good lr to each block.

title: Adaptive Memory Momentum via a Model-Based Framework for Deep Learning Optimization
author: Kristi Topollai et al
description: Most optimizers are first-order momentum-based. Oftentimes optimizers like AdamW rely on momentum coefficients like beta=0.9 that are kept constant during training. The authors introduce an adaptive memory that makes this momentum coefficient dynamic and adjusted online during the optimization, based on the gradient at current iteration and the other from the past memory.

title: RL Squeezes, SFT Expands: A Comparative Study of Reasoning LLMs
author: Kohsei Matsutani et al
description: This paper investigates the trajectory-level (looking at complete reasoning outputs) and step-level (looking a reasoning graphs whose nodes correspond to individual reasoning steps), and see that RL compresses incorrect trajectories, while SFT expands the correct ones. Basically, RL will concentrate the reasoning into a small subset of steps, while SFT will homogenize it across multiple steps.

title: Stochastic Rounding for LLM Training: Theory and Practice
author: Kaan Ozkara et al
description: This paper uses stochastic rounding to address numerical errors when training using low-precision, and analyze the convergence and regularization when using Adam optimizer + stochastic rounding.

title: SPAM: Spike-Aware Adam with Momentum Reset for Stable LLM Training
author: Tianjin Haung et al
description: A big problem in instability is from gradient and loss spikes, which lead to interventions like checkpoint recovery and experiment restarts. The authors present a comprehensive investigation into gradient spikes, and propose a spike-aware adam with momentum reset (SPAM), combatting gradient spikes via momentum reset and spike-aware gradient clipping.

title: SOAP: Improving and Stabilizing Shampoo using Adam
author: Nikhil Vyas et al
description: Shampoo has a drawback in that it includes additional hyperparams and computational overhead when compared to Adam. The authors show a connection between Shampoo and Adafactor, showing that Shampoo is equivalent to running Adafactor in the eigenbasis of Shampoo's preconditioner, and then present a simpler algorithm, ShampoO with Adam in the Preconditioner's

title: Scheduled Restart Momentum for Accelerated Stochastic Gradient Descent
author: Bao Wang et al
description: This paper proposes scheduled restart sgd (SRSGD) that replaces the constant momentum in SGD by the increasing momentum in nesterov accelerated gradient (NAG) but stabilies the iterations by resetting the momentum to zero on a schedule.

title: Pushing the Limits of Low-Bit Optimizers: A Focus on EMA Dynamics
author: Cong Xu et al
description: The authors present a method (SOLO) that enables Adam-style optimizers to maintain quantized states with precision as low as 3 or 2 bits. They are able to do this by resolving two problems: the "signal swamping problem" (the state may keep unchanged regardless of gap between newly received signal and current state value; this becomes a big problem when using a large momentum, beta->1, where the added value of (1-beta)_{z_{t}+1}} becomes very small and thus is easily swamped)in unsigned quantization, and the increased gradient variance in signed quantization that leads to incorrect descent directions. For the former, they suggest a logarithmic quantization, and for the later a precision-specific momentum hyperparam. SOLO can be applied to any Adam-style optimizer.

title: EM Distillation for One-Step Diffusion Models
author: Sirui Xi et al
description: The authors propose a maximum likelihood-based approach (EM Distillation, EMD) that distills a diffusion model to a one-step generator. Generator parameters are updated via Expectation-Maximization (EM), using samples from joint distribution of diffusion teacher prior and inferred generator latents. They alternate between an Expectation step that estimates the learning gradients with Monte Carlo samples and a Maximization-step that updates the student via gradient descent.

title: A Convergence Analysis of Adaptive Optimizers under Floating-point Quantization
author: Xuan Tang et al
description: The authors introduce a theoretical framework for analyzing convergence of adaptive optimizers such as Adam and Muon under floating-point quantization of gradients, weights, optimizer states. They show that such algorithms are able to maintain rates near their full-precision counterparts (as long as mantissa length scales logarithmically with number of iters). They note that Adam is highly sensitive to weights and second-moment quantization due to reliance on beta2 -> 1, while Muon does not have such constraints, thus being potentially more robust to quantization.

title: Mixture of Experts Provably Detect and Learn the Latent Cluster Structure in Gradient-Based Learning
author: Ryotaro Kawata et al
description: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of Mixture of Experts (MoE) trained with SGD on nonlinear regression tasks that have an underlying latent cluster structure, introducing a multi-phase training procedure where experts first weakly specialize, a router learns to partition data based on these signals, and experts then refine both local and global components. It proves that standard (single) neural networks fail in this setting because gradient interference cancels signals associated with shared structure, effectively increasing the problem’s information exponent and preventing recovery of certain features. In contrast, MoE succeeds by decomposing the task: individual experts weakly recover simpler cluster-specific functions, enabling the router to separate data and reduce interference, after which each expert can learn its assigned subproblem efficiently. The key insight is that MoE reduces effective learning complexity by aligning optimization with the latent data structure, turning a hard global problem into multiple easier local ones with favorable sample and runtime scaling.

title: Pruning is Optimal for Learning Sparse Features in High-Dimensions
author: Nuri Mert Vural et al
description: The paper studies neural networks for learning sparse high-dimensional feature models and introduces a prune-then-train procedure, where gradients at initialization are used to identify important input coordinates, reduce dimensionality, and then run gradient-based learning on the pruned subspace. It proves that when the true model directions are (soft) sparse, this pruning step aligns the network with the relevant features and enables gradient descent to achieve optimal sample complexity, matching sparsity-aware CSQ lower bounds. In contrast, standard “basis-independent” methods (e.g., randomly initialized dense networks) cannot exploit sparsity and therefore require strictly more samples, showing a formal separation. The key insight is that pruning is not just compression but a mechanism for feature selection via gradient signals, which reduces the effective dimension of the learning problem and makes otherwise hard high-dimensional estimation statistically optimal.

title: Skywork-MoE: A Deep Dive into Training Techniques for Mixture-of-Expert Language Models
author: Tianwen Wei et al
description: The paper studies how to effectively train Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models, focusing on the tradeoff between upcycling (initializing from dense checkpoints) and training from scratch, showing that the optimal choice depends on relative training budget and that large budgets favor scratch training due to better expert diversification. It identifies expert similarity as a key failure mode in upcycled MoEs, where identical initialization limits specialization, and proposes monitoring and encouraging diversification during training. To improve routing quality, it introduces gating logit normalization, which sharpens the softmax distribution of the router and prevents experts from behaving like uniform averages. Finally, it proposes adaptive auxiliary loss coefficients, dynamically adjusting load-balancing regularization per layer based on token routing statistics, so that load balancing does not overly constrain the main language modeling objective.

title: Neural network learns low-dimensional polynomials with SGD near the information-theoretic limit
author: Jason D. Lee et al
description: The paper shows that two-layer neural networks trained with SGD can learn single-index functions with sample complexity governed by the generative exponent 𝑝 ∗ p ∗ , rather than the larger information exponent 𝑝 p, by exploiting minibatch reuse during training. The key mechanism is that reusing the same data across gradient steps induces implicit nonlinear (polynomial) transformations of the labels, allowing SGD to access higher-order information beyond standard correlational statistical queries. This enables SGD to effectively simulate richer statistical query algorithms and reduce the effective learning difficulty, achieving near-optimal 𝑛 ∼ 𝑑 n∼d scaling for polynomial targets where 𝑝 ∗ ≤ 2 p ∗ ≤2. The analysis further shows that with appropriate training dynamics (layer-wise updates and normalization), SGD can progress from weak to strong feature recovery and ultimately learn both the latent direction and link function with near information-theoretic efficiency.

title: Diffusion Models are Minimax Optimal Distribution Estimators
author: Kazusato Oko et al
description: This paper provides a statistical learning theory for diffusion models by analyzing how well neural networks trained via score matching approximate the data distribution. Assuming the true density lies in a Besov space (a general class of functions that captures smoothness and includes Sobolev/Hölder spaces), the authors prove that diffusion models achieve nearly minimax-optimal estimation rates—i.e., they are statistically as efficient as possible up to constants—in both total variation distance (a measure of global distribution difference) and Wasserstein-1 distance (a transportation-based metric measuring the cost of moving mass between distributions). A key contribution is a construction that connects neural network approximation of time-dependent score functions (gradients of log-density) to final distribution error bounds, yielding explicit rates that depend on smoothness and sample size. The theory also shows that under a manifold (low intrinsic dimension) assumption, these rates depend on intrinsic rather than ambient dimension, meaning diffusion models can avoid the curse of dimensionality in such settings.

title: Learn from Your Mistakes: Self-Correcting Masked Diffusion Models
author: Yair Schiff et al
description: The paper introduces Progressive Self-Correction (ProSeCo), a framework that augments masked diffusion models (MDMs) with the ability to both generate (unmask) and revise (correct) tokens within the same model. It formulates model predictions as noisy samples of the true data and adds a self-correction cross-entropy objective that trains the model to recover from its own errors, with shared parameters between denoising and correction. The method provides a minimal modification to standard MDM training by incorporating an additional forward pass using sampled model outputs, along with a principled weighting scheme aligned with the diffusion objective. At inference time, it proposes an interleaved predictor–corrector sampling procedure that iteratively refines entire sequences, including already generated tokens, enabling dynamic error correction during generation.

title: L3: Large Lookup Layers
author: Albert Tseng et al
description: This paper introduces the Large Lookup Layer (L3), a new sparse architectural component that generalizes token embedding tables into intermediate transformer layers, enabling context-dependent computation via attention over token-specific embeddings. Unlike Mixture-of-Experts, L3 uses static token-based routing, eliminating the need for learned routers and enabling more hardware-friendly training, inference, and parameter offloading. The authors propose an information-theoretic embedding allocation method based on LZW-style compression to assign varying numbers of embeddings per token, approximating context-dependent routing using frequency of token patterns. Overall, L3 defines a new axis of sparsity that separates routing from computation, allowing models to cache and reuse information efficiently within learned embedding tables.

title: DUEL: Exact Likelihood for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Unmasking
author: Gilad Turok et al
description: DUEL reframes masked diffusion models (MDMs) as any-order autoregressive models with a deterministic unmasking policy, showing that a model is defined jointly by a denoiser and a position-selection rule. The key theoretical result is that when the unmasking policy is deterministic, the intractable marginalization over all generation orders collapses to a single trajectory, enabling exact likelihood computation under the test-time distribution. This yields a proper notion of perplexity for MDMs—directly analogous to autoregressive models—resolving fundamental issues with ELBO-based and sample-based evaluation. More broadly, the framework formalizes how different unmasking strategies induce distinct distributions and provides a unified way to analyze and compare them via exact likelihood.

title: dUltra: Ultra-Fast Diffusion Language Models via Reinforcement Learning
author: Shirui Chen et al
topic: dllm, diffusion, rl, unmasking, policy, grpo
description: Basically this paper uses GRPO to learn unmasking strategies that allow for more parallelization during decoding. Basically they add a head (takes last layer representations) that can plan how to denoise, and jointly optimize with GRPO. The main reason for RL is that the training data only includes greedily sampled trajectories, so we want some method that can explore teacher model modes while training on samlpes generated by student; this can be achieved by on-policy RL bc it trains on trajectories generated by the current policy, avoids distribution mismatch that occurs with offline distillation, and can explore via RL better unmasking strategies.

title: Likelihood-Based Diffusion Language Models
author: Ishaan Gulrajani et al
description: One of the earlier works on scaling diffusion llms. They trained the Plaid model.

title: Distillation of Discrete Diffusion through Dimensional Correlations
author: Satoshi Hayakawa et al
description: The paper identifies that standard discrete diffusion uses product-form denoisers (factorized over dimensions), which are scalable but cannot capture joint dependencies, forcing accurate modeling to rely on many sampling steps. To address this, it introduces a mixture-of-products model, where the denoiser is an expectation over multiple product distributions indexed by a latent variable 𝜆; although each component is factorized, the mixture is expressive enough to represent arbitrary joint distributions and thus capture dimensional correlations with minimal extra cost. For distillation, the method trains a student to match a multi-step teacher using a distillation loss (KL divergence between teacher and student one-step denoisers at small noise levels) combined with a consistency loss, which enforces that applying the student after a teacher step matches applying the student directly—thereby transferring the correlations implicitly formed by multi-step composition. Together, this setup learns to approximate the teacher’s composed multi-step distribution in a few steps, with theoretical guarantees that the loss functions upper-bound the discrepancy between student and teacher distributions.

title: Demystifying MaskGit Sampler and Beyond: Adaptive Order Selection in Masked Diffusion
author: Satoshi Hayakawa et al
description: The paper shows that the MaskGIT sampler implicitly performs temperature sampling, since its Gumbel-top-k selection makes positions and tokens behave as if drawn from sharpened distributions (p_i^beta) with (beta = 1 + 1/alpha), explaining both its effectiveness and its degradation with many steps. It introduces the moment sampler, which makes this explicit by first selecting positions based on expected confidence ((|p_i|_beta^beta)) and then sampling tokens directly from the temperature-scaled distribution (p_i^beta). This reformulation turns MaskGIT’s implicit “sample-then-choose” procedure into a clearer choose-then-sample (CTS) framework with separated index selection and token sampling. Building on this, the paper proposes partial transformer caching and a hybrid exploration–exploitation unmasking strategy to improve efficiency within the CTS view.

title: Learning Unmasking Policies for Diffusion Language Models
author: Metod Jazbec et al
description: The paper formulates masked diffusion sampling as a Markov Decision Process, where unmasking decisions are treated as actions and the diffusion model acts as the environment. It introduces a lightweight transformer-based policy that takes token confidence scores as input and outputs Bernoulli decisions over which positions to unmask, avoiding direct dependence on model hidden states. The policy is trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization using a reward that jointly captures correctness and computational efficiency via a multiplicative design. Overall, the work replaces handcrafted confidence-based heuristics with a learned, adaptive unmasking strategy that dynamically decides both the order and number of tokens to reveal during generation.

title: Information-Theoretic Discrete Diffusion
author: Moongyu Jeon et al
description: This paper gives an information-theoretic view of discrete diffusion by introducing Information–Minimum Denoising Score Entropy (I-MDSE) and Information–Minimum Denoising Cross-Entropy (I-MDCE), which formalize how information is lost during the forward noising process: as noise increases, the mutual information (I(x_0; x_t)) between the original data (x_0) and its corrupted version (x_t) decreases, and these identities show that the rate of this decrease is exactly given by the optimal denoising loss (DSE or DCE). Using this, they derive that the data log-likelihood (i.e., (log p(x_0)), the model’s assigned probability to a data point) can be written as an integral over noise levels of these optimal denoising losses, meaning likelihood is fully determined by how well the model can denoise at each step. This proves that common training losses (DSE for general discrete diffusion and DCE for masked diffusion) are not loose bounds but exact, principled estimators of likelihood when optimized. The framework also shows equivalence between score-based and masked diffusion via reparameterization and derives practical estimators such as a time-free likelihood formula, conditional likelihood decomposition, and low-variance coupled estimators for likelihood ratios.

title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
author: Peiyi Wang et al
description: The paper shows that using large language models (e.g., GPT-4) as evaluators introduces a strong positional bias, where simply changing the order (some models prefer first positions - GPT4, other prefer second ChatGPT) of candidate responses can flip evaluation outcomes. To mitigate this, it proposes Multiple Evidence Calibration (MEC), which forces the model to generate multiple pieces of evaluation reasoning before assigning scores, reducing reliance on a single biased judgment. It further introduces Balanced Position Calibration (BPC), which evaluates responses in both possible orders and averages the results to cancel out position effects. Finally, it adds Human-in-the-Loop Calibration (HITLC), which uses an entropy-based criterion to selectively involve humans on uncertain or biased cases, improving reliability while minimizing annotation cost.

title: d2: Improved Techniques for Training Reasoning Diffusion Language Models
author: Guanghan Wang et al
description: The paper derives a policy gradient objective for masked diffusion language models (DLMs), emphasizing that reinforcement learning requires computing likelihood ratios of generated outputs, which in turn depend on the model’s true probability structure. Unlike autoregressive models that factorize sequence likelihoods into per-token terms, DLMs define probabilities over entire denoising trajectories, where each token depends on intermediate noisy states across multiple steps. As a result, using per-token likelihoods (as in AR RL) produces biased gradients, since it does not match the model’s actual generative process, making trajectory likelihood estimation necessary for correct RL updates. To address this, the paper introduces d2-AnyOrder, an exact estimator that recovers a tractable factorization via any-order decoding and computes trajectory likelihoods in a single forward pass. Because most existing DLMs do not satisfy the assumptions required for this decoding scheme, the paper also proposes d2-StepMerge, which approximates trajectory likelihoods by grouping diffusion steps and trading off compute for bias with a theoretical error bound. Together, these contributions provide a principled RL framework for DLMs that replaces prior biased or intractable likelihood estimators with exact or controllably approximate trajectory-based methods.

title: XLNet: Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding
author: Guanghan Wang et al
description: In standard autoregressive models, causal attention fixes a single left-to-right order, so each token only attends to earlier tokens. XLNet instead samples a random permutation of token indices and treats that permutation as the factorization order, meaning “earlier” and “later” are defined by the permutation, not the original position. During training, attention masks are constructed so that a token can only attend to tokens that come before it in the sampled permutation, even though the underlying sequence order and positional encodings remain unchanged. Because different permutations place different tokens before or after a given position, the same token will sometimes see tokens to its left and sometimes to its right across training. In expectation over many permutations, each token learns to incorporate information from all other positions, effectively capturing bidirectional context while still using a causal (masked) attention mechanism. This works because causality is enforced with respect to the sampled permutation order, not the natural left-to-right order, so the model remains autoregressive but no longer tied to a single directional context.

title: VeriThinker: Learning to Verify Makes Reasoning Model Efficient
author: Zigeng Chen et al
description: The paper introduces VeriThinker, a method for compressing chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning without relying on synthetic short reasoning traces. Its core idea is Supervised Verification Fine-Tuning (SVFT), where a reasoning model is trained on an auxiliary binary task: given a question and a candidate CoT solution, predict whether the solution is fully correct. This reframes overthinking as a miscalibrated internal correctness classifier, and directly improves the model’s ability to decide when self-reflection is necessary rather than imitating shorter reasoning chains. Technically, SVFT uses a verification dataset of (question, solution, correctness label) and applies loss only on fixed verification responses, making the training effectively a contrastive-style binary classification over solution correctness. This improved verification capability reduces redundant reasoning steps while preserving critical error-checking, leading to adaptive control of self-reflection during inference. The paper further extends this idea to solution-wise speculative reasoning, where a fast short-CoT model proposes full solutions and the SVFT-enhanced model verifies them, only invoking expensive long-CoT reasoning when necessary.

title: Speculative Thinking: Enhancing Small-Model Reasoning with Large Model Guidance at Inference Time
author: Wang Yang et al
description:
The paper proposes Speculative Thinking, a training-free inference framework where a large model selectively guides a smaller model during reasoning rather than at the token level. The key idea is to identify structural cues in generation (especially paragraph breaks like “

” followed by reflective phrases) that signal moments of uncertainty or self-correction. At these points, the system dynamically hands control to a stronger model, which generates improved reasoning segments before returning control to the smaller model. The method defines three intervention mechanisms: handling reflection/affirmation segments, assisting explicit verification steps, and interrupting excessive self-reflection loops. This design is motivated by the observation that small models overproduce inefficient reflective reasoning, while larger models perform more concise and effective backtracking. Overall, the contribution is a hybrid inference strategy that injects targeted high-quality reasoning into small models without requiring retraining or modifying model weights.

title: Causality in Video Diffusers is Separable from Denoising
author: Xingjian Bai et al
description: The paper studies autoregressive video diffusion models and shows that temporal causal reasoning is not inherently tied to the multi-step denoising process, despite current architectures entangling the two. Through probing, it identifies two empirical regularities: early and middle denoiser layers produce highly similar features across denoising steps (indicating redundancy), while deeper layers exhibit sparse cross-frame attention and mainly perform intra-frame rendering. Based on these findings, the authors propose Separable Causal Diffusion (SCD), which decouples temporal reasoning from denoising by introducing a causal transformer encoder that runs once per frame and a lightweight diffusion decoder that performs per-frame iterative refinement without cross-frame attention. The encoder summarizes past frames into a latent context using causal attention with KV caching, and this context is reused across all denoising steps for the current frame, while the decoder conditions on this context to predict diffusion velocities for frame-wise denoising. The model is trained end-to-end with a standard diffusion objective combined with next-frame prediction, and includes techniques like context corruption to mitigate exposure bias and improve robustness. This design reduces redundant computation by amortizing temporal reasoning and eliminating repeated cross-frame attention during denoising, while maintaining compatibility with both training-from-scratch and finetuning-from-pretrained diffusion setups.

title: CoT-Valve: Length-Compressible Chain-of-Thought via Parameter-Space Control
author: Xinyin Ma et al
topic: cot, length control, parameter space, interpolation, extrapolation
description: The paper introduces CoT-Valve, a parameter-space method for controlling chain-of-thought length by learning a task vector Δθ that modulates reasoning verbosity without changing final answers. This vector is implemented via a LoRA branch whose scaling coefficient α enables interpolation (long ↔ short reasoning) and extrapolation to lengths outside the training distribution. To support this, the authors construct MixChain, a dataset where each question is paired with multiple reasoning chains of varying lengths generated by sweeping α, avoiding repeated sampling. They propose two training refinements: CoT-Valve++ enforces consistency across different α values by conditioning on normalized length factors, and CoT-Valve+P performs progressive compression by training on gradually shorter chains. The framework treats length control as a continuous direction in parameter space, enabling localized and fine-grained compression beyond prompt-based methods. Overall, the contribution is a unified tuning and inference scheme that turns reasoning length into a controllable dimension via task arithmetic rather than discrete prompting or post-hoc pruning.

title: Sparse-dLLM: Accelerating Diffusion LLMs with Dynamic Cache Eviction
author: Yuerong Song et al
description: The paper identifies that diffusion LLMs exhibit persistent attention sparsity and stable token saliency across decoding steps, meaning only a small subset of tokens consistently receives high attention. Based on this, it proposes Sparse-dLLM, a training-free inference framework that selectively evicts low-importance KV cache entries instead of storing full states. The method introduces dynamic bidirectional cache eviction, which prunes both prefix and suffix tokens outside the current decoding block using attention scores computed between current queries and cached keys. It further incorporates a delayed cache update mechanism, postponing KV caching by one step to avoid instability in early decoding states. The approach uses attention pooling and top-k selection with a retention ratio to identify and preserve salient tokens, enabling structured sparsification of the cache. Overall, the contribution is a plug-and-play inference strategy that exploits temporal consistency in attention to reduce computation while maintaining effective context representation.

title: dInfer: An Efficient Inference Framework for Diffusion Language Models
author: Yuxin Ma et al
description: The paper introduces dInfer, a modular inference framework for diffusion language models that decomposes inference into four components: model, diffusion iteration manager, decoding strategy, and KV-cache manager. It proposes an iteration smoothing algorithm that carries forward token representations across denoising steps to improve contextual consistency and mitigate degradation from caching. For decoding, it adds hierarchical decoding to recursively partition spans and ensure progress, and credit decoding to accumulate confidence across iterations and prioritize stable token predictions. To address the incompatibility of standard KV caching with bidirectional attention, it introduces a vicinity KV-cache refresh strategy that selectively recomputes keys and values for tokens near the active decoding region while keeping others cached. The framework also incorporates system-level optimizations including combined tensor and expert parallelism, CUDA graph execution, and loop unrolling to eliminate kernel launch overhead across diffusion steps. Additionally, it supports trajectory distillation to train models using effective decoding trajectories, improving their ability to exploit parallel decoding during inference.

title: Introspective Diffusion Language Models
author: Yifan Yu et al
description: The paper identifies a core weakness in diffusion language models: they lack introspective consistency, meaning the tokens they generate are often not supported when the model later re-evaluates them under a standard causal prediction rule. Formally, a token is sampled from a generation distribution q, but when the model computes the corresponding causal distribution p, the same token may have low probability, so the model does not “agree with itself.” In contrast, autoregressive models inherently enforce p = q because both generation and evaluation use the same next-token objective, which leads to stable and self-consistent reasoning. The authors argue that this mismatch in diffusion models causes error accumulation and weak long-horizon coherence, since the model cannot reliably validate its own intermediate outputs. To fix this, they propose Introspective Diffusion Language Models, which align q and p through causal masking, logit shifting, and a training objective that jointly enforces generation and verification consistency. At inference time, their decoding algorithm generates multiple tokens in parallel while verifying them against the causal distribution, ensuring that accepted tokens are consistent with what the model itself would predict.

title: DMax: Aggressive Parallel Decoding for dLLMs
author: Zigeng Chen et al
description: The paper identifies that diffusion language models fail under aggressive parallel decoding because they use a one-way mask-to-token process, where early incorrect predictions are fixed and then propagate, causing error accumulation without any mechanism for revision. To address this, the authors reformulate decoding as a self-refinement process in embedding space, where tokens are not immediately committed but can be iteratively updated. Their first key component, On-Policy Uniform Training, converts a masked diffusion model into a self-correcting model by training it not only on masked inputs but also on its own sampled predictions, using on-policy rollouts to reduce the mismatch between training and inference distributions. Concretely, they generate noisy sequences by sampling model predictions at masked positions, run two forward passes on masked and predicted sequences, and optimize both with cross-entropy against the clean target so the model learns to denoise its own mistakes. The second component, Soft Parallel Decoding, replaces discrete token states during inference with hybrid embeddings that interpolate between the predicted token embedding and the mask embedding based on prediction confidence, preserving uncertainty and enabling iterative correction. Combined with a block-wise decoding scheme that promotes tokens based on confidence thresholds and re-evaluates all positions until convergence, this allows the model to revise earlier predictions and maintain stability even under highly parallel updates.

title: Stable-DiffCoder: Pushing the Frontier of Code Diffusion Large Language Model
author: Chenghao Fan et al
topic: dllm, block diffusion, training, masking, warmup, noise schedule
description: The paper addresses the gap that diffusion language models for code have not matched autoregressive models in capability under equal data and compute, despite offering advantages like parallel generation and data reuse through masking. The authors argue the core issue is inefficient knowledge learning caused by random masking producing many weak or misleading contexts, where the correct token is underdetermined or noisy, reducing the ability to learn clear reasoning rules. To fix this, they propose a training pipeline that starts from an autoregressive checkpoint to efficiently compress “token reasoning knowledge,” then applies small-block diffusion continual pretraining so the model learns multi-token prediction while preserving strong, well-aligned contexts. Technically, their block diffusion masks only short contiguous spans (e.g., size 4) while keeping surrounding tokens clean, ensuring the conditional prediction problem remains well-constrained and aligned with inference-time block decoding. They further stabilize training with a warmup strategy that gradually increases masking ratio and removes diffusion loss reweighting early on, and introduce a block-wise clipped noise schedule that enforces at least one masked token per block to avoid zero-signal updates. Overall, the solution reframes diffusion training as a controlled data augmentation process that balances context diversity with learnability, ensuring that additional masking patterns improve rather than degrade knowledge acquisition.

title: In-Place Test-Time Training
author: Guhao Feng et al
topic: tts, fast weights, mlp, chunk-wise updates, learning objective
description: The paper introduces In-Place Test-Time Training, a method that allows large language models to adapt during inference by updating a subset of parameters called fast weights instead of remaining fully static. The key design is to reuse the existing MLP block by treating its final projection matrix as fast weights, updating it in-place while keeping the rest of the model frozen, which enables compatibility with pretrained models without architectural changes. To address inefficiency, the method replaces sequential per-token updates with chunk-wise updates, where tokens are processed in blocks and each block produces a single gradient-based update to the fast weights, allowing parallel computation. The core methodological contribution is a new learning objective aligned with next-token prediction, where targets incorporate future token information using a shifted convolution over embeddings instead of reconstructing the current token. Theoretical analysis shows that this objective increases the logit of the correct next token while leaving other logits mostly unchanged under standard assumptions, whereas reconstruction objectives do not improve predictive accuracy.
