Overall, the work formulates test-time training as a scalable and theoretically grounded mechanism for embedding a dynamic memory into existing transformers through localized weight updates.

title: Rethinking Video Generation Model for the Embodied World
author: Yufan Deng et al
description: The paper identifies a core limitation in current video generation models for embodied AI: existing evaluation frameworks focus on perceptual quality and fail to capture task correctness and physical realism in robotic scenarios. To address this, it introduces RBench, a benchmark that formalizes robotic video evaluation along two axes, task completion and visual quality, with fine-grained sub-metrics such as physical-semantic plausibility, task adherence consistency, motion amplitude, robot-subject stability, and motion smoothness, many of which are implemented via MLLM-based VQA protocols over temporally sampled frames. The benchmark is constructed from 650 curated image-text pairs spanning five task categories and four robot embodiments, with careful design to avoid data leakage and ensure alignment with realistic robotic action semantics. The evaluation methodology explicitly models common physical failure modes such as non-contact motion, object drift, and action omission, thereby operationalizing physical realism as measurable criteria rather than relying on proxy visual metrics. Complementing evaluation, the paper proposes RoVid-X, a large-scale dataset built through a four-stage pipeline consisting of large-scale video collection, quality filtering, task segmentation with automated captioning, and physical property annotation including optical flow and depth estimation. The overall contribution is a coupled framework where benchmark design defines the target notion of physical intelligence and the dataset construction pipeline injects the corresponding inductive biases, enabling scalable training and rigorous assessment of video models for embodied robotic behavior.

title: Seedance 2.0: Advancing Video Generation for World Complexity
author: Bytedance Seed et al
description: The paper introduces Seedance 2.0, a unified multimodal audio-video generation model that shifts the paradigm from short, weakly controlled video synthesis to highly controllable generation conditioned on text, images, audio, and video. The core contribution is a single large-scale architecture for joint audio-video generation that integrates multimodal reference inputs and editing operations, enabling both generation and transformation tasks within one framework. The model is designed to explicitly handle real-world complexity by improving temporal coherence, motion stability, and physical plausibility through better modeling of dynamics across frames. It incorporates structured multimodal conditioning that allows fine-grained control over subject identity, motion, style, cinematography, and narrative sequencing, effectively embedding a form of implicit “directorial reasoning” into the generation process. A key technical aspect is the integration of synchronized audio generation, including multi-track and binaural audio, with strict temporal alignment to visual content, treating audio-visual generation as a single coupled problem rather than separate modules. Additionally, the system supports compositional workflows such as reference-based generation, targeted editing, and video continuation, reflecting a design that unifies generation and editing under consistent conditioning and alignment mechanisms.

title: Coupling Experts and Routers in Mixture-of-Experts via an Auxiliary Loss
author: Ang Lv et al
topic: moe, router, expert specialization, auxiliary loss
description: The paper identifies a core limitation in Mixture-of-Experts models: the router is trained without explicit constraints to align its decisions with the true capabilities of experts, leading to suboptimal routing and weak specialization. It proposes an expert-router coupling (ERC) loss that treats router embeddings as cluster centers and uses perturbed versions of these embeddings as proxy tokens representing the distribution of inputs assigned to each expert. These proxy tokens are fed through all experts, and the L2 norms of intermediate activations are collected into an n by n matrix that measures how strongly each expert responds to each proxy. The ERC loss enforces two asymmetric constraints: each expert must respond most strongly to its own proxy compared to others, and each proxy must activate its corresponding expert more than any other, effectively coupling routing representations with expert functionality. This is implemented as a hinge-style penalty over off-diagonal activation entries relative to scaled diagonal terms, with a hyperparameter controlling the strictness of specialization. To ensure proxies remain representative of their assigned token clusters, bounded multiplicative noise is applied to router embeddings, with the bound derived from distances between cluster centers to avoid crossing decision boundaries. The method has constant computational cost in the number of experts rather than tokens, preserving sparse MoE efficiency while providing an explicit and tunable mechanism for aligning routing behavior with expert specialization.

title: There Will be a Scientific Theory of Deep Learning
author: Jamie Simon et al
description: The authors argue that a scientific "learning mechanics" is emerging—a mathematical theory describing neural network training that parallels how physics describes physical systems through laws of motion and interaction. The centerpiece is the lazy/rich dichotomy: initialization scale fundamentally determines whether networks exhibit feature learning or behave linearly, revealing that seemingly small hyperparameter choices steer networks toward qualitatively different regimes. The Discretization Hypothesis proposes that finite networks are essentially noisy approximations to infinite-width/depth limits, suggesting the complexity of deep learning conceals underlying mathematical simplicity when viewed at the right scale. The authors identify universal phenomena across diverse architectures and datasets—different models converge to similar representations and follow predictable power-law scaling laws—indicating that a relatively simple theory might explain disparate systems. Finally, they position learning mechanics as complementary to mechanistic interpretability, with one serving as physics and the other as biology: learning mechanics explains how mechanisms form during training through first-principles dynamics, while mechanistic interpretability reveals what those mechanisms do semantically.

title: Learning Dynamics of LLM Finetuning
author: Yi Ren et al
description: This paper presents a unified learning dynamics framework for understanding LLM finetuning by decomposing how model predictions change at each training step into three mathematical components: an adaptation matrix that describes how the output probability distribution responds to parameter changes, an empirical neural tangent kernel that measures how similar different input-response pairs are from the model's perspective, and a gradient term that captures the direction and strength of the update based on the specific loss function being used. The framework extends prior work to handle the sequential nature of language models through causal masking, which prevents the model from looking at future tokens when making predictions, enabling analysis of how learning from one response influences predictions on other responses. The authors identify and formally characterize a "squeezing effect" that occurs when large negative updates are applied to low-probability predictions in softmax-based models, where the probability mass removed from rejected tokens concentrates on the already most-likely tokens rather than spreading evenly across other possibilities. This effect explains counter-intuitive phenomena observed in off-policy DPO such as declining confidence across all responses and the emergence of repeated phrases, while also explaining why on-policy variants perform better than their off-policy counterparts. The theoretical analysis leads to a simple algorithmic improvement where training on both preferred and rejected responses during the supervised finetuning stage elevates rejected responses before preference tuning, thereby reducing the squeezing effect during subsequent DPO training.

title: Stochastic Collapse: How Gradient Noise Attracts SGD Dynamics Towards Simpler Subnetworks
author: Feng Chen et al
topic: theory, sgd, implicit bias, invariant sets
description: The paper introduces invariant sets as subsets of parameter space that remain unmodified by SGD updates and identifies two fundamental classes: sign invariant sets corresponding to sparse subnetworks with vanishing neurons and permutation invariant sets corresponding to low-rank subnetworks with identical neurons. These invariant sets arise from reflection symmetries inherent in neural network architectures, formalized through Theorem 3.1 which establishes that any loss function approximately symmetric around an affine subspace generates an invariant set. The authors establish the concept of stochastic attractivity to characterize when SGD dynamics are drawn toward invariant sets, deriving a sufficient condition (Theorem 4.2) that reveals a fundamental competition between the loss landscape's curvature around an invariant set and the magnitude of gradient noise introduced by stochastic updates. A key theoretical insight is that increased gradient noise strengthens attraction to invariant sets, counterintuitively enabling convergence to saddle points or local maxima of the training loss rather than true minima. In the linear teacher-student framework, the authors prove that stochastic collapse acts as an implicit regularization mechanism that prevents the model from learning small-signal modes, thereby reducing overfitting and improving generalization. The theory mechanistically explains why maintaining large learning rates for extended periods during early training benefits subsequent generalization: larger learning rates increase gradient noise, driving stronger stochastic collapse toward simpler subnetworks that are subsequently less prone to overfitting. The analysis unifies the understanding of SGD's implicit bias by demonstrating that the interplay between architectural symmetries and stochastic gradient noise naturally biases learning dynamics toward parameter-efficient solutions.

title: Neural Ordinary Differential Equations
author: Ricky T. Q. Chen et al
topic: theory, continuous depth, adjoint method, normalizing flows, time-series
description: The paper introduces neural ordinary differential equations, which parameterize the derivative of hidden states using a neural network and compute outputs through black-box ODE solvers instead of using discrete layers. The key technical contribution is the adjoint sensitivity method, which enables reverse-mode automatic differentiation through ODE solvers by solving an augmented ODE backwards in time without storing intermediate forward pass operations, achieving constant memory cost independent of depth. The authors derive the instantaneous change of variables formula, which states that the log probability change under continuous transformations follows a differential equation depending only on the trace of the Jacobian, avoiding the cubic-cost determinant computation of discrete normalizing flows. This trace linearity enables continuous normalizing flows with many hidden units at linear computational cost, as the total trace is simply the sum of individual traces. The framework naturally supports time-dependent dynamics and gating mechanisms by parameterizing flow parameters as functions of time. For time-series modeling, the approach treats irregular sampling and missing data naturally through continuous latent dynamics that define trajectories between arbitrary time points without requiring fixed binning or discretization. The method allows explicit trade-offs between numerical accuracy and computational speed by adjusting ODE solver tolerances, with faster solutions achievable during inference by reducing precision requirements. The entire framework integrates ODE solvers as black-box components into larger differentiable models through vector-Jacobian products, enabling end-to-end training without access to solver internals.

title: The Hidden Width of Deep ResNets: Tight Error Bounds and Phase Diagram
author: Lenaic Chizat et al
topic: resnet, ode, mean-field, scaling, training dynamics
description: This paper shows that infinite-depth ResNets converge to a Neural Mean ODE limit as depth L diverges, regardless of the actual hidden width M, establishing a fundamental connection between depth and width in residual network training dynamics. A Neural Mean ODE is a continuous-depth limit model parameterized by a stochastic process Z(s) over depth s ∈ [0,1], where the forward pass satisfies ∂_s h(s,x) = E[ϕ(h(s,x), Z(s))], taking an expectation over the parameter distribution at each depth level to obtain a deterministic ODE that individual finite-depth ResNets approximate through stochastic sampling of their weights. The authors prove error bounds of O(1/L + σ₀/√(LM)) between finite-depth ResNets and their infinite-depth limits, decomposing the error into depth-discretization and sampling components with effective width ML. The paper identifies two distinct training regimes: a maximal local update (MLU) regime where features evolve significantly, and a lazy ODE regime where the mean ODE is linearized and feature updates vanish. For ResNets with two-layer perceptron blocks, they derive that the critical residual scale O(√D/(LM)) is necessary and sufficient for achieving maximal local feature updates while maintaining stability. The theoretical framework leverages stochastic approximation theory combined with propagation of chaos arguments, showing that random initialization causes forward and backward passes to behave as stochastic approximations of mean ODEs. By propagation of chaos, the asymptotic independence of units is preserved throughout training, enabling control of the finite-depth dynamics relative to the infinite-depth limit. These results bridge classical mean-field neural network analysis with neural ODE perspectives and extend optimal hyperparameter scaling principles from finite-width to the infinite-depth regime.

title: On the Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Over-parameterized Models using Optimal Transport
author: Lenaic Chizat et al
topic: optimal transport, mean-field, particle gradient descent, homogeneity, initialization
description: This paper studies particle gradient descent for minimizing convex functions over the space of measures, a framework that encompasses sparse deconvolution and training neural networks with a single hidden layer. The key contribution is showing that, despite the non-convexity of the finite-dimensional particle problem, gradient flows converge to global minimizers of the original convex problem in the many-particle limit when properly initialized. The authors reformulate the problem via a lifting trick that turns weights into just another coordinate of particle positions, removing the asymmetry between weights and positions and exposing a homogeneity structure that is central to their analysis. They characterize the many-particle limit of the particle gradient flow as a Wasserstein gradient flow, leveraging optimal transport theory, and prove existence and uniqueness of this limit under mild regularity assumptions. Two main theoretical settings are analyzed: a 2-homogeneous case (covering ReLU networks) where initialization must separate two concentric spheres, and a partially 1-homogeneous case (covering sigmoid networks and sparse deconvolution) where initialization must separate two parallel hyperplanes in the lifted domain. The proofs combine homogeneity properties with a separation property of the support that is preserved throughout the dynamics, allowing the flow to escape neighborhoods of non-optimal stationary points. The authors apply these abstract results to derive concrete initialization prescriptions for sparse deconvolution, sigmoid networks, and ReLU networks, noting that standard ReLU parameterization has discontinuous differentials and proposing an equivalent reparameterization using signed squared weights to recover differentiability. The work provides a consistency principle showing that commonly used non-convex training methods inherit global optimality from the underlying convex structure, with limits in time and particle number being interchangeable rather than requiring a specific relative growth rate.

title: The merged-staircase property: a necessary and nearly sufficient condition for SGD learning of sparse functions on two-layer neural networks
author: Emmanuel Abbeet al
topic: theory, sgd, mean-field, sparse functions, fourier analysis
description: This paper characterizes which functions can be efficiently learned by stochastic gradient descent (SGD) on two-layer neural networks in the mean-field regime when the target function depends on a low-dimensional latent subspace. The main contribution is the introduction of the merged-staircase property (MSP), a hierarchical condition on the Fourier coefficients of a function that can be ordered such that consecutive sets differ by at most one element, and the paper proves this property is both necessary and nearly sufficient for learning with O(d) sample complexity in d-dimensional ambient space. The authors establish a dimension-free dynamics framework that approximates the standard mean-field dynamics for sparse functions and show that a function is strongly SGD-learnable if and only if this simplified dynamics can reach zero risk. The near-sufficiency result holds for generic MSP functions (excluding measure-zero degenerate cases with symmetric invariances), while vanilla staircase functions of the form z_1 + z_1z_2 + ... + z_1z_2...z_P are shown to be learnable without any genericity assumption. A key theoretical contribution is proving that non-linear training is necessary, as linear methods including neural tangent kernels require superpolynomial sample complexity for these functions. The proof techniques reduce global convergence analysis to a polynomial identity testing problem and provide improved lower bounds for kernel methods that are tighter for non-orthogonal function classes. The paper demonstrates a fundamental separation between SGD-trained networks and linear methods for MSP functions, showing the former can achieve O(d) sample complexity while the latter require at least polynomial in d. These results provide the first tight characterization of what functions regular neural networks can learn via standard SGD in a regime that goes beyond the linear regime.

title: High-dimensional dynamics of generalization error in neural networks
author: Madhu S. Advani al
topic: eigenspectrum, generalization, early stopping, random matrix theory
description: The paper analyzes generalization error dynamics in neural networks within the high-dimensional regime where the number of parameters approaches or exceeds the number of training samples, using random matrix theory to derive exact solutions for linear models. The authors show that generalization error depends critically on the eigenvalue spectrum of the input correlation matrix, which is characterized by the Marchenko-Pasteur distribution—a mathematical law describing how eigenvalues of large random matrices are distributed, predicting worse overtraining when the number of parameters equals the number of samples. They introduce the concept of a "frozen subspace" consisting of weight directions where no learning occurs under gradient descent, which provides implicit regularization independent of model size. For deep linear networks, they derive a dimensionality reduction that compresses the coupled nonlinear dynamics into a small set of equations coupled only through a global scalar, revealing how depth affects training speed. The authors demonstrate that early stopping combined with small-norm weight initialization nearly matches optimal L2 regularization performance and that the optimal stopping time grows approximately logarithmically with the signal-to-noise ratio. They derive an alternative Rademacher complexity bound—a traditional measure from learning theory that quantifies how easily a model can fit random labels—that incorporates the frozen subspace and eigenspectrum properties, showing this modified bound qualitatively matches observed behavior where generalization improves with network size above the sample count. The theory reveals that large overcomplete networks naturally protect against overfitting because the increasing eigengap between zero and the smallest nonzero eigenvalue limits weight growth regardless of stopping time. These results suggest that traditional worst-case Rademacher complexity bounds fail to capture generalization in neural networks because they ignore how gradient descent dynamics select solutions with limited weight norms through the interplay of initialization, early stopping, and the statistical properties of high-dimensional data.

title: Language Model Circuits Are Sparse in the Neuron Basis
author: Aryaman Arora al
description: The paper challenges the conventional belief that neural network neurons are not sparse by demonstrating that MLP neurons can serve as an equally sparse basis for circuit tracing as learned features from sparse autoencoders. The authors identify two key methodological improvements: using MLP activations rather than MLP outputs as the representation basis, and replacing Integrated Gradients with RelP, a more efficient attribution method requiring only a single backward pass. MLP activations function as a privileged basis due to element-wise nonlinearity in the MLP architecture, which encourages features to align with individual neuron dimensions. RelP improves upon Integrated Gradients by linearizing nonlinearities while maintaining local faithfulness to the original model, enabling cleaner and faster attribution computations. The neuron basis offers advantages over learned feature bases because it avoids reconstruction errors and polysemanticity issues inherent to sparse autoencoders, and requires no additional computational overhead during model training. The authors develop an end-to-end circuit tracing pipeline that uses gradient-based attribution to identify causal circuitry in the neuron basis without requiring external dictionary learning. Their approach demonstrates that neurons can be directly interpreted through automatic neuron descriptions and circuit analysis, making them a practical alternative to more complex learned representations. This work suggests that interpretability research should more thoroughly investigate the neuron basis before defaulting to learned feature decompositions.

title: The Optimization Landscape of SGD Across the Feature Learning Strength
author: Alexander Atanasov al
description: This paper investigates how the feature learning strength parameter γ affects stochastic gradient descent optimization in neural networks trained online, where γ is a hyperparameter that scales the final layer output by a factor of (γ√N)^{-1} (N being network width) under the maximal update parameterization (µP) framework. The fundamental insight is that γ directly controls the trade-off between "lazy" learning (where networks behave like fixed kernel methods) and "rich" feature learning (where networks adaptively learn new representations): as γ increases from near zero toward one and beyond, the network transitions from using fixed features to progressively learning new features throughout training. The authors identify a characteristic phase portrait in the γ-η plane showing that optimal learning rates follow distinct power-law scalings depending on the regime: η* ∝ γ^2 when γ ≪ 1 (lazy regime where feature learning is suppressed), and η* ∝ γ^2/L when γ ≫ 1 (ultra-rich regime where feature learning is pronounced) for networks of depth L. This non-trivial γ dependence of optimal learning rates emerges from the Hessian structure, which scales as γ^{-2} in the lazy regime but transitions to γ^{-2/L} scaling in the rich regime, reflecting how differently the loss landscape changes based on whether the network is learning features. The authors develop a minimal linear network model explaining how γ-dependent dynamics arise, showing that in the ultra-rich regime (γ ≫ 1) networks must grow their weights substantially to minimize loss, fundamentally altering the optimization trajectory compared to the lazy regime where parameters barely move from initialization. Networks in the large γ regime exhibit characteristic phenomena including stepwise loss drops and progressive sharpening, behaviors that become more pronounced as γ increases and reflect deeper feature learning. Remarkably, networks with different large γ values optimize along dynamically equivalent trajectories when time is rescaled by τ = ηt/γ, suggesting that γ controls a fundamental scaling of the feature learning timescale rather than qualitatively different learning mechanisms.

title: High-dimensional Asymptotics of Feature Learning: How One Gradient Step Improves the Representation
author: Jimmy Ba et al
topic: theory, feature learning, high-dimensional asymptotics, gaussian equivalence, spike models
description: This paper studies what happens to a two-layer neural network when you take just one gradient descent step on the first-layer parameters (the weights that transform the input) in a specific mathematical regime where the training set size, input dimensions, and network width all grow proportionally together. The main contribution is showing that this single gradient step creates a special "spike" direction in the weight matrix that aligns with the linear component of the function being learned, a phenomenon related to what mathematicians call the Baik-Ben Arous-Péché phase transition. The authors extend an important theoretical tool called the Gaussian Equivalence Theorem to handle trained features instead of just random features, allowing precise calculations of prediction performance. Under small learning rates, they prove the learned features improve upon random features but cannot defeat the best purely linear model. However, under large learning rates that scale with network width, they show that certain learned features can actually surpass the theoretical limits of standard kernel methods and outperform many classical approaches. The surprising insight is that even a single gradient step provides substantial benefit, demonstrating that feature learning in early training phases is fundamentally important. The analysis relies on discovering that the gradient matrix itself has an approximate low-rank structure, which simplifies the mathematical treatment. Overall, this work is novel in rigorously explaining why and how neural networks improve over fixed kernels through feature learning in the high-dimensional limit where data size and model size are comparable.

title: Explaining Neural Scaling Laws
author: Yasaman Bahri et al
topic: theory, scaling laws, kernel methods, data manifold, duality
description: This paper proposes a unified theory explaining why neural networks show predictable power-law scaling relationships between their performance (measured as loss or error) and either the amount of training data or the number of model parameters. The theory identifies four distinct scaling regimes by combining two factors - scaling with dataset size and scaling with model size - with each regime driven by different underlying mechanisms. In the variance-limited regime, which occurs when either dataset size or model size is very large compared to the other, the paper proves that performance improvement follows a universal scaling exponent of one, meaning the error decreases proportionally to one divided by the dataset size or one divided by model width, independent of the specific architecture or dataset type. In the resolution-limited regime, which occurs when one factor (dataset size or model size) is constrained while the other grows large, the scaling exponents depend on the intrinsic dimensionality of the data manifold, essentially the minimum number of geometric dimensions needed to represent the actual underlying structure of the data. A surprising finding is the discovery of a duality showing that the way model size affects performance in the data-abundant regime mathematically mirrors the way dataset size affects performance in the model-abundant regime, suggesting these superficially different problems are fundamentally related. The theoretical insights derive from analyzing simple linear models built from random features, which behave mathematically identically to very wide neural networks in the infinite-width limit, enabling precise derivation of scaling relationships from the spectrum (the collection of eigenvalues) of kernel matrices. The paper establishes that for smooth data distributions, the spectrum of kernel functions determines the scaling exponents through their connection to the manifold's geometric dimensionality, connecting classical mathematical results to modern deep learning. A key insight is that neural networks appear to learn properties of the input data structure largely independent of the specific classification task, potentially explaining why deep learning representations transfer so effectively to different downstream applications.

title: Revisiting Model Stitching to Compare Neural Representations
author: Yamini Bansal et al
topic: theory, model stitching, representation similarity, feature learning
description: The paper revives model stitching, a method where you take the lower layers of one trained neural network and connect them to the upper layers of another using a simple trainable connector layer, based on the logic that neural networks process information sequentially—lower layers learn simple features like edges and textures, while upper layers combine these into complex patterns needed for the final task—so if you swap one model's lower layers into another's upper layers and performance stays the same, it means both models learned equally useful features. The authors argue that model stitching is better than existing similarity metrics like CKA (centered kernel alignment) because it focuses only on features that matter for the final task rather than all features including useless or random ones, and it can identify truly different representations versus ones that are just rearranged. They introduce the concept of "stitching connectivity," which means different neural networks trained separately with different random starting points can have their layers swapped with minimal performance loss, suggesting they learn essentially the same internal structures. A surprising finding is that this stitching connectivity appears to be a fundamental property of how stochastic gradient descent (the standard training algorithm) works, meaning most trained models can be connected to each other even though they have completely different weights. They provide evidence for the intuitive idea that "all roads lead to Rome," showing that networks trained very differently (like one using supervised learning and another using self-supervised learning without labels) can exchange their early layers without significant performance loss. They also validate the intuition that "more is better," demonstrating that representations learned by models trained with more data, larger size, or longer training time can successfully replace those in weaker models and actually improve their performance. A key advantage of model stitching is that it produces results in the same units as the task itself, like accuracy percentage, making it easy to interpret what differences actually mean in practical terms. The surprising insight is that despite neural networks having vastly different internal parameters, the actual learned features they develop tend to be remarkably compatible and composable, which reveals something fundamental about how deep learning works.

title: Statistical physics of deep learning
author: Jean Barbier et al
topic: theory, statistical physics, replica method, specialization transitions, order parameters
description: This paper develops a theoretical framework for understanding how multi-layer neural networks learn when the number of training examples is comparable to the number of trainable parameters, a challenging setting that previous theories could not adequately address. The authors use statistical physics methods, specifically combining the replica method from spin glass theory with the HCIZ integral (a mathematical tool for analyzing matrix models), to derive exact formulas describing what features a trained network learns as more data becomes available. A key discovery is that learning happens through specialization transitions, where individual hidden neurons gradually align their weights with the target network's features, and these transitions do not occur simultaneously across all layers but rather propagate from inner to outer layers in a hierarchical manner. The paper introduces order parameters, which are mathematical quantities that track how much alignment between student and teacher weights occurs, revealing that deeper layers require exponentially more data to learn than shallower ones. The authors prove surprisingly that two distinct learning phases exist: an initial universal phase where the network learns without specializing, followed by a specialization phase where optimal performance is achieved through weight alignment. A novel methodological contribution is their unified treatment of matrix degrees of freedom that do not have rotational symmetry, solving a longstanding problem in theoretical physics that previous approaches could not handle. An important and somewhat surprising finding is that even when theoretical predictions show an optimal solution exists, practical training algorithms like gradient descent often get trapped in suboptimal solutions due to metastable states that act as attractive barriers. The framework successfully predicts which features are learned when, providing a quantitative map of the learning process that goes far beyond previous theoretical understanding of deep learning.

title: Consistent Diffusion Language Models
author: Hasan Amin et al
topic: dlm, diffusion models, consistency, stochastic bridges, multi-path training
description: This paper introduces Consistent Diffusion Language Models (CDLM), a new framework for accelerating discrete diffusion models by replacing the missing deterministic probability-flow ODE with a stochastic alternative based on exact posterior bridges between noise levels. The key idea is Multi-Path Discrete Consistency (MPDC), which trains a denoiser to produce consistent predictions in expectation across many possible stochastic paths connecting noisy and clean states, rather than along a single trajectory. Concretely, given a noisy state xt, the model is trained so that its direct prediction matches the expected prediction obtained by first sampling an intermediate state xs from the exact posterior bridge q(xs | xt, x0) and then denoising from xs, enforcing path-invariance. This yields a training objective where the model becomes a fixed point of a consistency operator defined over the true reverse process, with an additional “diffusion anchor” term that ties predictions to real data and prevents degenerate solutions. The framework unifies several existing approaches, showing that masked diffusion, continuous consistency models, and distillation methods can all be interpreted as special cases or approximations of the same bridge-based consistency principle. As a result, CDLM learns to decompose long denoising steps into equivalent shorter transitions, enabling reliable few-step generation as an intrinsic property of training rather than requiring multi-stage distillation.

title: Back to Basics: Let Denoising Generative Models Denoise
author: Tianhong Li et al
topic: diffusion, denoising, x-prediction, manifold assumption, transformers
description: This paper argues that modern diffusion models deviate from true denoising by predicting noise (ϵ) or mixed quantities (v), and instead proposes to directly predict clean data (x-prediction) as a fundamentally different and more appropriate objective. The core insight is based on the manifold assumption: natural data lie on a low-dimensional manifold, while noise occupies the full high-dimensional space, so predicting noise requires much higher model capacity than predicting clean data. The authors show that when operating in high-dimensional spaces (e.g., large pixel patches), ϵ- and v-prediction fail because the network must model high-dimensional off-manifold structure, whereas x-prediction remains tractable by focusing only on the low-dimensional signal. Building on this, they introduce a minimal architecture called “Just image Transformers” (JiT), where a standard Vision Transformer directly maps noisy images to clean images without tokenizers, latent spaces, or auxiliary losses. They formalize that different prediction targets (x, ϵ, v) are mathematically interchangeable via reparameterization, but empirically not equivalent due to capacity and optimization constraints, making the choice of prediction target critical in practice. Overall, the paper reframes diffusion modeling as a representation learning problem, showing that directly predicting clean data enables simple, self-contained Transformer-based diffusion models to scale to very high-dimensional inputs without architectural complexity.

title: Sparser, Faster, Lighter Transformer Language Models
author: Edoardo Cetin et al
topic: gpu, sparsity, feedforward layers, custom kernels, activation sparsity
description: The paper addresses a key inefficiency in Transformer-based language models: most of the computation in feedforward layers is wasted because many neuron activations are effectively zero. Although this sparsity is naturally present, exploiting it has historically been difficult because GPUs are optimized for dense, regular computation patterns rather than irregular sparse operations. To resolve this, the authors introduce a new sparse representation called TwELL (Tile-wise ELLPACK), which organizes non-zero activations in a way that aligns with GPU tiling and avoids costly synchronization or memory overhead. They combine this format with custom CUDA kernels that fuse multiple operations—such as projection, sparsification, and recomputation—into a single execution pipeline, minimizing data movement and kernel launches. During training, sparsity is encouraged using a simple combination of ReLU activations and L1 regularization on intermediate activations, which can push sparsity levels above 99% without significantly degrading model accuracy. To handle the uneven distribution of sparsity across tokens and layers, they also introduce a hybrid format that selectively stores highly active rows in dense form while keeping most others compressed. Empirically, this system achieves meaningful efficiency gains, including roughly 20–30% speed improvements, reduced GPU memory usage, and lower energy consumption, especially at larger model scales. Overall, the paper demonstrates that activation-level sparsity can be a practical and scalable optimization for large language models when both the data representation and compute kernels are co-designed for modern GPU architectures.

title: Decoding-Free Sampling Strategies for LLM Marginalization
author: David Pohl et al
topic: marginalization, importance sampling, tokenization lattice, subword language models
description: The paper studies how to approximate the marginal probability of a surface text under a subword language model, i.e., summing probabilities over all possible tokenizations, without incurring the high cost of autoregressive decoding required by importance sampling. It proposes a decoding-free approach that constructs a tokenization lattice (a DAG encoding all valid subword segmentations of the text) and samples tokenizations directly from this structure, enabling efficient enumeration and uniform sampling without invoking the model’s generation process. The key idea is that scoring complete token sequences can be parallelized in a single forward pass, while generation requires sequential passes, so replacing sampling-from-model with sampling-from-lattice dramatically reduces cost while still approximating the marginal. To improve approximation quality, the method prioritizes high-probability regions by explicitly enumerating tokenizations close to the canonical segmentation (e.g., local token splits) and then sampling additional sequences under length constraints, yielding a monotonic lower-bound estimate of the marginal that efficiently captures most of the probability mass.

title: Diffusion-State Policy Optimization for Masked Diffusion Language Models
author: Daisuke Oba et al
topic: policy optimization, masked language models, denoising models
description: The paper asks whether policy optimization for masked diffusion language models can exploit intermediate denoising states to achieve finer-grained credit assignment, instead of attributing a single terminal reward to all token decisions. It proposes Diffusion-State Policy Optimization (DISPO), which treats each intermediate masked sequence as a state and the simultaneous filling of its masked positions as an action, then performs same-state branching by resampling multiple fillings from cached logits and scoring their resulting completions with the same terminal reward. The key technical idea is to define a fixed-state policy gradient objective using a masked-token surrogate likelihood restricted to newly filled positions, enabling unbiased likelihood-ratio updates that isolate credit to actionable tokens while reusing rollout data without additional diffusion steps. This is effective because comparing counterfactual completions conditioned on the same intermediate state reduces variance and disentangles local decision quality, turning intermediate logits already produced during diffusion into meaningful, state-conditioned supervision signals.

title: Drifting Objectives for Refining Discrete Diffusion Language Models
author: Daisuke Oba et al
description: The paper asks whether the quality of discrete diffusion language models under a fixed number of denoising steps can be improved by modifying the training objective rather than the sampler, and proposes to import drifting-based fixed-point learning from continuous generative models. The core difficulty is that DDLMs output categorical distributions, so direct drifting in feature space is non-differentiable; the solution is a soft-token lift that maps token probabilities to expected embeddings, enabling a differentiable path from logits → softmax → embedding mixture → frozen encoder features. In this feature space, the method constructs an anti-symmetric attraction–repulsion drift field using real and generated feature neighborhoods, and defines a stop-gradient target h* = sg(h + αV), so minimizing the L2 distance induces a gradient proportional to −V that pushes model features toward data regions and away from model-induced modes. This works because the soft-token representation preserves uncertainty and allows meaningful geometry in feature space, while the anti-symmetric drift ensures a fixed point when model and data distributions match, making the objective both stable and aligned with improving sample quality directly rather than token reconstruction.

title: Best-of-∞ – Asymptotic Performance of Test-Time LLM Ensembling
author: Junpei Komiyama et al
description: The paper studies the asymptotic limit of majority-voting best-of-N inference for LLMs, which the authors call best-of-∞, and asks two coupled research questions: how to approximate this idealized limit efficiently with finite test-time compute, and how to optimally combine multiple LLMs through weighted ensembling under such voting. To approximate the limit efficiently, the authors model the unknown distribution over an LLM's answers with a Dirichlet process prior DP(H, α), which gracefully handles the fact that an LLM may emit a variable and a priori unknown number of distinct answers per problem, with the concentration parameter α encoding prior belief in yet-unseen answers and unifying the treatment of finite and infinite answer supports. They then derive a Bayes factor comparing the hypothesis that the currently most-frequent answer is the population majority against its negation, approximate the DP posterior by a Dirichlet distribution over observed answers with an extra residual component of mass α for unseen outcomes, and estimate it via Monte Carlo sampling so generation can stop per problem as soon as evidence exceeds a threshold, naturally spending more samples on harder problems and fewer on easy ones. For ensembling, sampling LLM i with weight w_i makes each problem's voted answer deterministic in the N → ∞ limit, namely the answer j maximizing Σ_i w_i p_{i,j} where p_{i,j} is the probability LLM i emits answer j, which collapses what at finite N would be an enumeration over exponentially many vote outcomes into a single linear condition per problem. The key technical insight is that the set of weight vectors under which a given answer wins is an intersection of half-spaces Σ_i w_i (p_{i,j} − p_{i,j'}) > 0 with the simplex and is therefore a polytope, so maximizing the total number of correctly-voted problems reduces exactly to a mixed-integer linear program with binary indicators y_q signaling whether problem q's correct-answer polytope contains w, solvable at realistic scales with off-the-shelf MILP solvers. Because the optimal region is a polytope rather than a single point, the authors further refine the solution by selecting a max-margin interior point via binary search over a margin parameter ξ that tightens each half-space without reducing the achievable objective, which empirically improves finite-N behavior even though all interior points are equivalent in the strict best-of-∞ limit.

title: Stopping Computation for Converged Tokens in Masked Diffusion-LM Decoding
author: Daisuke Oba et al
description: The paper tackles a specific inefficiency in masked diffusion language models: at every denoising step they recompute self-attention and per-token feed-forward sublayers for all N positions, including already-unmasked tokens whose predictions have effectively stopped changing, leaving the per-step attention cost stuck at O(N²d) even late in sampling when most positions are settled. SureLock addresses this by permanently locking a position once its posterior has stabilized, defined by the per-step KL divergence between consecutive posteriors falling below a threshold ε (optionally combined with a confidence gate selecting the top-m% most peaked active tokens); a locked position skips its Q-projection and FFN forever, while its keys and values are cached so other still-active tokens can continue to attend to it, which contracts the active set monotonically and turns the dominant cost into O(MtNd) with Mt shrinking as sampling proceeds. The choice of local KL as the locking signal is justified by a closed-form bound that telescopes per-step logit differences across steps after the lock time t*: under assumptions of geometric tail contraction of the KL (Ds ≤ ρDs−1), one-step logit smoothness tying logit drift to √D, and Lipschitzness of log-softmax, the deviation between the locked terminal log-probability and the counterfactual no-lock log-probability is bounded by Ctail·√(D at lock time), giving the explicit design rule ε(δ) = δ²/Ctail² for a target terminal error δ. This makes SureLock orthogonal to prior efficiency axes that either reduce the number of diffusion steps or reuse intermediate K/V across steps, because those still issue N query rows per step, whereas SureLock changes the question from what to recompute now to what to remove from compute permanently, so its savings compound on top of step-count or reuse-based methods rather than competing with them.

title: Improved Techniques for Training Consistency Models
author: Yang Song et al
description: The paper asks how to make consistency training (CT) — where a single-step generative model is learned directly from data — competitive with consistency distillation, while also eliminating the dependence on the LPIPS perceptual metric that causes evaluation bias (since LPIPS and the FID Inception network share ImageNet features) and adds compute overhead. The authors first identify a flaw in the original CT theory: prior work argued the CT loss converges to the consistency matching loss as the number of discretization steps N grows, but the authors prove via a Dirac-data toy example that when the teacher's EMA decay rate is nonzero (so θ⁻ ≠ θ), the limiting objective collapses to a term independent of the data and provides no learning signal, whereas setting μ = 0 makes the rescaled gradient converge to a proper mean-squared-error gradient toward the true consistency function — so they remove EMA from the teacher entirely. To replace LPIPS they adopt the Pseudo-Huber metric √(‖x−y‖² + c²) − c, which smoothly interpolates between ℓ1 and squared ℓ2, remains twice differentiable as the CT convergence proofs require, and is more robust to large errors than squared ℓ2, which empirically reduces the variance of Adam parameter updates that otherwise plagues CT because the bootstrapped teacher target is noisy. They further reweight the loss by 1/(σ_{i+1} − σ_i) so lower noise levels — whose errors propagate upward through the consistency chain — dominate, sample noise levels from a discretized lognormal (Pmean = −1.1, Pstd = 2.0) instead of the original schedule that over-weighted large σ and ignored that the boundary-condition parameterization already makes small-σ learning easy, and grow N exponentially via N(k) = min(s0·2^⌊k/K′⌋, s1) + 1 up to 1281, trading off the bias from finite-N discretization against the variance that explodes when adjacent noise levels become too close.

title: Bootstrap Your Own Latent A New Approach to Self-Supervised Learning
author: Jean-Bastien Grill et al
topic: latent, self-supervised learning, contrastive learning, representation learning
description: BYOL addresses whether self-supervised image representation learning can avoid negative pairs, which contrastive methods rely on to prevent collapse but which require large batches, memory banks, or careful mining and make performance heavily dependent on augmentation choices. The solution uses two networks: an online network (encoder, projector, predictor) trained to predict the projection of a target network applied to a different augmented view of the same image, where the target network's weights are an exponential moving average of the online weights and gradients only flow through the online branch via a stop-gradient. Although predicting in representation space without negative examples admits trivial collapsed solutions, the authors argue collapse is avoided because (1) the asymmetric predictor on the online side, when near-optimal, makes the loss approximate the conditional variance of the target projection given the online projection, and reducing this variance requires retaining information rather than discarding it, and (2) the slow-moving target network ensures the predictor stays close to optimal across training while still propagating new sources of variability from the online network into the targets, so the dynamics are not joint gradient descent on any single loss (analogous to GANs) and the constant-output equilibrium is unstable. This bootstrapping interpretation is reinforced by the finding that the target network can be removed entirely if the predictor is forced to remain near-optimal (e.g., via a closed-form linear regression solution or a much higher predictor learning rate), suggesting the moving-average target's main role is to keep the predictor near-optimal rather than to provide stable negative-free signal per se.

title: Consistent Diffusion Models: Mitigating Sampling Drift by Learning to be Consistent
author: Giannis Daras et al
description: Diffusion models suffer from sampling drift: because the learned score function is imperfect, samples generated during the reverse process drift away from the true noisy distributions p_t that the model was trained on, and since the standard Denoising Score Matching (DSM) objective only optimizes the score on non-drifted (in-distribution) samples, errors compound recursively as t decreases. The paper asks how to improve the score estimate on off-distribution inputs without direct access to the true score, and proposes enforcing a consistency property on the denoiser h(x,t) = E[x_0 | x_t = x], which requires that h's prediction at (x,t) equals the expected value of x_0 obtained by initializing the model's own reverse SDE at x_t = x and running it backward to t=0. This works because, by Tweedie's formula, h and the score s are linearly related, and the authors prove that if h is consistent and its induced score is a conservative field, then s must satisfy the same PDE (derivable from Fokker-Planck via Ito's lemma applied to the reverse-Martingale condition on h) that any true diffusion score satisfies, so by uniqueness of the PDE, matching s* on just one time t_0 and an open subset of R^d forces s = s* everywhere (using analyticity of Gaussian-smoothed densities). Practically, since enforcing full-trajectory consistency is expensive, the authors exploit the equivalent local reverse-Martingale form from Lemma 3.1, sampling t and a nearby t' in [t-ε, t], drawing x_{t'} from the model's conditional SDE in a few discretization steps, and penalizing the squared difference between h(x_t, t) and h(x_{t'}, t'), which is cheap because local consistency implies global consistency. This consistency loss is added as a regularizer to the standard DSM objective, so the model is simultaneously anchored to the true score on in-distribution points by DSM and propagated correctly to off-distribution regions by the consistency constraint, addressing exactly the drift regime where DSM provides no signal.

title: Infinite Mask Diffusion for Few-Step Distillation
author: Jaehoon Yoo et al
description: The paper studies why masked diffusion models (MDMs) fail in few-step generation, identifying a structural lower bound on factorization error caused by the use of a single deterministic mask token, which forces independent per-token predictions when multiple masked tokens are decoded simultaneously. This limitation arises because the reverse model factorizes over tokens, while the true posterior contains inter-token dependencies, and with a single mask state there is no latent variability to encode joint structure, leading to irreducible conditional total correlation. To address this, the authors propose the Infinite Mask Diffusion Model (IMDM), which replaces the single mask token with an infinite set of stochastic mask states, implemented by injecting continuous noise into the mask embedding while maintaining strict separability from data tokens. This stochastic mask introduces latent variability that can act as a coordination variable, enabling a partition-and-map mechanism where different noise realizations deterministically map to different coherent token configurations, thereby matching the true joint distribution and theoretically allowing zero factorization error. The key insight is that by expanding the mask state space and preserving the masked/unmasked distinction, IMDM retains the simplicity and training objective of MDMs while restoring the capacity to model joint dependencies during parallel decoding, which is critical in the few-step regime.

title: ELF: Embedded Language Flows
author: Keya Hu et al
description: The paper asks whether the underperformance of continuous diffusion language models is intrinsic to language discreteness or instead due to suboptimal design, and proposes Embedded Language Flows (ELF) to test the latter by removing most discrete constraints. ELF maps tokens into a continuous embedding space and performs generation entirely as a continuous-time flow matching process, where noisy embeddings follow a linear interpolant z_t = t x + (1 − t) ε and the model predicts clean embeddings x via an x-prediction objective that induces the correct velocity field. A key design choice is to avoid any token-level supervision during intermediate steps, allowing unconstrained trajectories in embedding space, and to defer discretization to a single final step implemented by a shared network that switches from denoising to decoding mode. This unification is technically important because predicting clean embeddings aligns the denoising objective with the final cross-entropy decoding loss, enabling weight sharing and eliminating the need for a separate decoder while preserving consistency between continuous and discrete representations. Because the model operates purely in continuous space until the end, it naturally supports techniques like classifier-free guidance and stochastic sampling, which improve controllability and error correction by modifying the learned velocity field during integration.

title: Where Should Diffusion Enter a Language Model? Geometry-Guided Hidden-State Replacement
author: Injin Kong et al
topic: dlm, diffusion models, geometry, hidden states
description: The paper asks where diffusion should be applied inside a pretrained transformer, hypothesizing that failure of continuous diffusion in language stems less from discreteness and more from using representation spaces with unfavorable geometry for denoising. It formalizes “diffusion-friendly” spaces via three properties—fast contraction under Langevin dynamics (linked to curvature), robustness to score estimation error (also controlled by curvature), and low effective dimensionality (captured by effective rank)—and derives these as practical spectral proxies computed layer-wise from hidden states. Using these proxies, it scores each transformer layer and selects an insertion point where hidden-state distributions are locally compact, globally well-conditioned, and lie near low-dimensional manifolds, making them easier targets for diffusion. The proposed DiHAL architecture then replaces the lower transformer layers with a conditional diffusion bridge trained to reconstruct the selected hidden state from noise, while keeping the upper layers and LM head unchanged so that token prediction is handled by the original autoregressive machinery. This design avoids unstable continuous-to-discrete recovery by shifting diffusion to an internal representation that the pretrained model already knows how to decode, effectively reframing language diffusion as hidden-state reconstruction in a geometrically suitable space.

title: SparseD: Sparse Attention for Diffusion Language Models
author: Zeqing Wang et al
description: The paper addresses the high inference latency of diffusion language models by asking how to reduce the quadratic cost of bidirectional attention without breaking the denoising process. It observes that, unlike autoregressive models, attention in DLMs is head-specific but temporally stable across diffusion steps, and that early denoising steps are disproportionately important for forming correct global structure. Based on this, SparseD first runs full attention during early steps, then computes head-specific sparse attention patterns by selecting high-score query–key pairs (separately for prefill and generation tokens) using blockwise pooled attention, and reuses these fixed patterns for all subsequent steps. This works because the denoising trajectory stabilizes attention structure after early steps, so recomputing patterns each step is redundant, and restricting computation later does not significantly perturb the trajectory. The method reduces complexity by masking attention to a precomputed sparse set while preserving critical early global interactions, effectively amortizing the cost of pattern discovery and aligning sparsification with the temporal dynamics of diffusion.

title: Elastic-dLLM: Position Preserving Context Compression and Augmentation of Diffusion LLMs
author: Junyi Wu et al
topic: dlm, mask tokens, rope, context compression, iterative folding
description: The paper asks whether the large computational cost in diffusion language models comes from repeatedly processing many future [MASK] tokens that contribute little new information, and how to reduce this redundancy without breaking generation. It shows that although [MASK] tokens have highly redundant feature representations and mostly interact locally with each other, they encode critical structural signals through their RoPE positional indices, especially for determining where content should be generated and when to terminate. Based on this, the method compresses the future [MASK] context by sparsely sampling tokens from each block while keeping decoded history, the current block, and the terminal region dense, and crucially preserves each retained token’s original RoPE coordinate so that positional priors remain unchanged. This design works because generation in dLLMs is strongly guided by positional encoding rather than token identity for masked positions, so maintaining the positional “skeleton” preserves semantics even under heavy token reduction. The framework further extends to iterative folding, where older decoded tokens are also sparsified under the same positional-preserving rule for long-context scaling, and to block dLLMs by adding a single protected [MASK] token at the final RoPE position to explicitly reintroduce the terminal signal that guides EOS prediction.
